d: With the advent of supertall towers, and in some cases, articulating architectural features, the designing of façade access equipment has necessarily become increasingly complex. However, safety and user friendly designs are still the paramount considerations.

t: Burj Dubai: Life Safety and Crisis Response Planning Enhancements
l2: Jon M. Evenson &  Aaron F. Vanney, RJA
d: This paper will review the Crisis Response Plan and Life Safety enhancements that are currently being developed to assist the Burj Dubai Management Team in managing various crises. Readers will gain an understanding of the challenges facing the Burj Dubai when responding to crises, as well as proactive approaches that include both Life Safety Enhancements and Crisis Response Planning Enhancements.

t: Brief on the Construction Planning of the Burj Dubai Project, Dubai, UAE
l2: Ahmad Abdelrazaq, Kyung Jun Kim & Jae Ho Kim, Samsung C&T
d: The focus of this paper will be on the construction planning of the Burj Dubai tower, this paper will briefly present an overview of the structural system of the tower’s design and construction, which are integrated from the early design concept.

t: A Tale of Two Cities: Collaborative Innovations for Sustainable Towers
l2: John Lahey & Martin Wolf, Solomon Cordwell Buenz; Ron Klemencic & Ola Johansson, MKA
d: Policies in a number of American cities encourage increased residential density and height, as well as new standards for sustainability. However, creative and sensitive responses to the design, engineering, and cost challenges posed by this complex building type are critical to its long-term acceptance and success.

t: Provocations: Sustainable Architecture Today
l2: Robert F. Fox, Jr., Cook + Fox Architects
d: Through four case studies – 4 Times Square, the Battery Park City Guidelines, The Bank of America Tower at One Bryant Park, and PlaNYC2030 – this paper presents tangible ways in which we might begin to think, act and design differently.

t: Innovations in Sustainability at Height: Experimental Tall Buildings
l2: Peter Land, Illinois Institute of Technology
d: As global concern and competition intensifies over diminishing fossil fuel reserves, the need grows to conserve energy, develop renewable resources and more efficient ways to build. In a rapidly urbanizing world these objectives must be considered a top priority. Therefore, an experimental approach to the design development of high-rise structures, forms and energy systems should be promoted in the search for ways forward in the planning and design of the future built environment.

t: The Vertical Farm: The sky-scraper as vehicle for a sustainable urban agriculture
l2: Dickson Despommier, Columbia University; Eric Ellingsen, Illinois Institute of Technology
d: Vertical, urban farming in tall buildings involves fully sustainable energy use and creation in a new and literal organic relationship between engineering, architecture, technology, and global agricultural imperatives in local based community solutions.

t: Five Energy Generations of Tall Buildings: A Historical Analysis of Energy Consumption in High Rise Buildings
l2: Philip  Oldfield, University of Nottingham; Dario Trabucco, Universita IUAV di Venezia; Antony Wood, CTBUH
d: This paper aims to rectify this by examining the history of energy use in tall buildings, from their origins in North America in the late 19th century to the present day. In doing so, it categorises tall buildings into five chronological ‘generations’, based on their energy consumption characteristics.

t: NIST Metrics and Tools for Tall and Green Buildings
l2: Shyam Sunder & Barbara Lippiat, National institute of Standards and Technology
d: Tall building stakeholders need compelling metrics, tools, data, and case studies supporting major investments in sustainable building technologies. Proponents of green building widely claim these buildings to be cost-effective, but often these claims are based on incomplete, anecdotal evidence that is difficult to reproduce and defend.

t: Overview of Sustainable Design Factors in High-Rise Buildings
l2: Mir M. Ali &  Paul J. Armstrong, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
d: This paper examines the critical design factors and strategies that warrant consideration to accomplish sustainable or high-performance tall buildings applying innovative technologies.

t: Addressing India’s Local Concerns With A Sustainable Approach To Building Tall
l2: Sudhir Jambhekar & Pete Weingarten, Fox & Fowle Architects
d: In developing countries, places where builders are routinely confronted with challenges inherent to poor infrastructure, obstacles effecting constructability, and a lack of political will and state resources, the sustainable tall building provides a unique opportunity for economic analysis and social reflection.

t: Urban Development To Combat Climate Change: Dongtan Eco-city and Risk Management Stratagies
l2: Peter Head & Gary Lawrence, ARUP
d: Drawing on lessons learned from the planning and development of a new, low-carbon eco-city on an island opposite Shanghai in China, this paper explores the potential of an integrated urbanism approach.

t: Microclimate Study of a City in Hot and Humid Climate
l2: Seifu A. Bekele, Ian Jones and Gokul K. Rajamani, Vipac Engineers & Scientists Ltd.
d: The study shows that for Dubai and the surrounding area, the recommended street orientation may promote ventilation and increase the pollution dispersion. Shadows, combined with vegetation and climate-friendly city mass distribution, may be able to achieve the desired objective.

t: Greening Your Skyscraper: Case Study in Improving the Environmental Performance of an Existing Skyscraper
l2: Lester Partridge & Eoin Loughnane, Bassett Applied Research
d:
This paper outlines the strategies and design processes undertaken for refurbishing a 30 storey building
in Sydney’s CBD. The building was originally constructed in 1976 and consists of 35,500m2 commercial net lettable
area over 30 floors with ground floor retail tenancies

t: Green or Grey? The Aesthetics of Tall Building Sustainability
l2: Antony Wood, CTBUH
d: This paper outlines the viability of the tall building as a sustainable element in our future urban centres and examines several tall building case studies that have embraced environmental approaches to a lesser or greater degree.

t: Delivering Sustainable Tall Buildings
l2: Michael Deane, Turner Construction Company
d: This paper will cover the role of the builder in delivering sustainable tall buildings. Using projects completed by Turner as examples and citing data from Turner Construction’s Green Market Barometer Surveys, it will discuss the high degree of misperception on the part of decision makers about the true costs and benefits of green buildings which has until recently slowed the adoption of green buildings in the commercial market.

t: Oppressive Impact of High Rise Office Buildings on Inhabitants through an Istanbul Case Study
l2: Hande Ünlü & Kotaroh Hirate, The University of Tokyo; Munakata Jun, Chiba University
d: In this research, high-rise office buildings are categorized into three types by their “interaction generating factors” qualities. A questionnaire survey was held in Istanbul and it determined that interaction is one of the most significant factors for inhabitants to adapt themselves into this globalizing environment.

t: Rotating Tower Dubai
l2: David H. Fisher, Dynamic Architecture Group
d: The project presented is based on three fundamental concepts: Dynamic, Green Sustainability and industrially produced.

t: A Postcard from Dubai - Design and Construction of Some of the Tallest Buildings in the World
l2: Andy Davids, Jonathan Wongso, Darko Popovic & Angus McFarlane, Hyder Consulting Middle East
d: This paper presents an overview of the design and construction of some of the tallest buildings in the world, currently under construction in the Gulf region. The paper explores some of the key natural and commercial forces at work in the region, and how these forces shape the design of these super high rise projects.

t: Structural Sustainability in the Gulf – Fact and Fiction
l2: Kerry Galbraith, KEO International Consultants
d: The main objective of this paper is to educate developers, designers and contractors on the role of structural design in sustainable buildings and its implication within the gulf region.

t: Tall Buildings: Sustainable Design Opportunities
l2: Akbar Tamboli, Leonard Joseph, Umakant Vadnere & Xiao Xu, Thornton-Tomasetti Group
d: This paper describes three major tall buildings focusing on their incorporation of sustainable structural designs.

t: Sustainable Tall Buildings – Some Introductory Remarks
l2: Werner Sobek & Heiko Trumpf, Werner Sobek Group
d: This paper considers the basic requirements needed to achieve truly sustainable buildings. It discusses possible ways of modifying the cooperation between all project partners responsible for the implementation of a green design.

t: Towards a 21st Century Post Carbon Urbanised Society
l2: Lee Morris, W S Atkins & Partners Overseas
d: The first section of the paper will attempt to look at the current situation of mankind as the planet faces climate change, emissions targets and the search for new energy alternatives. The last section will attempt to hypothesise about what the future may look like with regards to energy, the built environment, technology, waste and how the human race adapts and embraces change.

t: Tall Buildings Sustainability from the bottom up
l2: Alistair Guthrie, Ove Arup Partners,
d: This paper examines the idea that a building can only be sustainable if it is so in the context of the community which uses it. It concludes that tall buildings can contribute to the sustainability of the community. It considers SPeAR as a way of measuring sustainability and provides an example of such measurement.

t: From the Tallest to the Greenest - Paradigm Shift in Dubai
l2: Habiba Al Marashi, UN Global Impact; Jasleen Bhinder, Emirates Environmental Group
d: This paper discuses the Urban Challenges facing Dubai due to the construction boom, including the social, economical and environmental impacts of the construction industry, Dubai’s Economic Gain due to this boom in the economy, and the efforts undertaken by the government to mandate the construction of green buildings.

t: Shanghai World Financial Center: Without Compromise……
l2: Paul Katz, KPF; Leslie Robertson & SawTeen See, Leslie E. Robertson Associates
d: Shanghai World Financial Center is located in the Lujiazui district of Pudong, the Shanghai World Financial Center combines creative architecture and structural engineering to create a mixed-use building 492 meters in height. It will be recognized by the CTBUH as the world’s tallest in two of its four categories when completed in April 2008; as the “Highest Occupied Floor” and “Top of Roof”.

t: Towards More Sustainable Tall Buildings
l2: Ken Dalton & Richard John, AECOM
d: The paper covers with the view that sustainability outcomes will only be fully successful if sustainability priorities are shared by the planners, investors, developers, landlords, occupants, and their respective supply chains.

t: Engineering the World’s Tallest - Burj Dubai
l2: William F. Baker, D. Stanton Korista & Lawrence C. Novak, SOM
d: The goal of the Burj Dubai Tower is not simply to be the world’s highest building; it’s to embody the world’s highest aspirations.

t: Burj Dubai: Designing the World's Tallest
l2: Adrian Smith, Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture
d: This paper discusses the process of the Burj Dubai's design, offering insights into the refinement and development of the design in response to programmatic requirements, structural wind-tunnel testing and other issues related to the design of a country’s landmark and the world’s tallest structure.

t: It’s Not What You Build, But the Place Where You Build It: Urban Sustainability in London
l2: Peter Rees, City of London Corporation
d: This paper argues that a wise property developer who wishes to avoid leaving a clumsy trail of carbon footprints across the planet will therefore choose sustainable development locations.

t: Chicago: Building a Green City
l2: Richard M. Daley & Sadhu Johnston, City of Chicago
d: Chicago has a tradition of innovation in architecture from balloon-frame houses to steel skyscrapers to today’s green buildings. Chicago was one of the first cities to adopt the U.S. Green Building Council’s LEED certification system and today Chicago has almost 200 LEED certified and registered projects more than any other city.

t: The Sustainable Vision of Dubai
l2: Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, H.E. Hussain Nasser Lootah, United Arab Emirates
d: This paper focuses on recent strategies implemented in Dubai that will improve the sustainable performance of the city’s built environment; specifically the green building directive outlined by HH Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum in October 2007 and the LEED Emirates assessment system, as developed by the Emirates Green Building Council.

t: Outrigger System Study for Tall Building Structure With Central Core and Square Floor Plate
l2: King-Le Chang, King-Le Chang & Associates; Chun-Chung Chen, Jaelien Engineering Consultant
d: This paper is to demonstrate the effectiveness of the outrigger system for a central cored building structure with two outrigger floors and perimeter columns, and also considers the slab stiffness at the typical floors.

t: Green Design of Residential High-Rise Buildings in Livable Cities
l2: Mir M. Ali & Paul J. Armstrong, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
d: This paper illustrates the interdependency of the sustainable design of high-rise residential buildings and their urban habitats. It argues that increased density is commensurate with the goals of sustainability and fosters a livable city with residences, goods, and services concentrated onto a smaller land area.

t: High-Rise Home Technology
l2: Brenda Calvin, The Calvin Group, LLC
d: Residential high-rise is currently witnessing a renaissance of exponential proportion in just about every major city in the world. However, many of these homes are not just a remake of their vertical ancestors. The high-rise homes being built today are much more “alive”, with living, breathing technology to enhance the owners’ experience of the lifestyle.

t: The “International” Skyscraper: Observations
l2: Georges Binder, Buildings & Data SA
d: While using tall buildings data, the following paper aims to show trends and shifts relating to building use and new locations accommodating high-rise buildings. After decades of the American office building being dominate, in the last twelve years we have observed a gradual but major shift from office use to residential and mixed-use for Tall Buildings, and from North America to Asia. The turn of the millennium has also seen major changes in the use of buildings in cities having the longest experience with Tall Buildings. Chicago is witnessing a series of office buildings being transformed into residential or mixed-use buildings, a phenomenon also occurring on a large scale in New York. In midtown Manhattan of New York City we note the transformation of major hotels into residential projects. The transformation of landmark projects in midtown New York City is making an impact, but it is not at all comparable to the number of new projects being built in Asia. When conceiving new projects, we should perhaps bear in mind that, in due time, these will also experience major shifts in uses and we should plan for this in advance.

t: The Race for the Sky: Unbuilt Skyscrapers
l2: Mohamad Kashef, East Carolina University
d: Unbuilt skyscraper designs are noted by many architecture critics as the best in the genre of tall buildings. This paper provides an exposition of various iconic and “unbuilt” skyscraper design proposals that have inspired architects and engineers to build ever-taller buildings and continue to fuel the 21st century race for the sky.

t: Four Tall Buildings in Madrid – Study of the Wind-Induced Responses
l2: Peter Paul Hoogendoorn & Pamon Alvarez Cabal, INTEMAC
d: Limiting wind-induced building motion is one of the governing criteria in tall building design.  Building motion should be limited, on the one hand, to prevent damage in partitions, facade elements and interior finishes.

t: Integration of Dynamic Facades with other Building Systems
l2: A. Tzempelikos & A. K. Athienitis, CABA
d: Driven by technological advances in transparent building facades and the general motivation for high quality green buildings, façade design alternatives have shifted to utilizing dynamic fenestration and shading systems for optimal control of daylight and solar gains.

t: Tallest Buildings Completed in 2007
d: 333 meters high with 72 stories and 480 suites, Rose Rotana Tower in Dubai leads the list of the 10 tallest buildings completed in 2007. The tower, which is developed by Bonyan International Investment Group, is also regarded as the world’s tallest single-use hotel building.

t: Structural BIM: Discussion, Case Studies and Latest Developments
l2: Clive Robinson, Tekla
d: This paper discusses the current state of building information modelling (BIM) from the structural point of view, incorporating the migration from 2D to 3D solutions.

t: The Effects of Complex Geometry on Tall Towers
l2: David Scott, David Farnsworth, Matt Jackson & Matt Clark, Arup
d: Advancements in the application of computational capabilities to the design and analysis of building structures has enabled the realization of tall buildings with complex geometries. This paper explores some of the design issues and tools.

t: Editorial
l2: Antony Wood, CTBUH
d: Editorial written by Antony Wood for the Wiley Tal Journal 2007.

t: Green Specs / LEED Specs
l2: Mark Kalin, Kalin Associates Inc.
d: Which products are green, greener, greenest? Are you conversant with LEED requirements, FSC-certified wood, CRI-certified carpet, synthetic gypsum, and commissioning? Specifications communicate the designer’s intent to the contractor. Our workshop leader is the author of the original GreenSpec.

t: The Economics of Supertall Towers
l2: Watts, Steve; Kalita, Neal & Maclean, Michael, Davis Langdon
d: This paper addresses global economic factors infl uencing the decisions leading to the development of super-tall towers, including population growth and urbanization, economic cycles and the ‘Skyscraper Index’, regulation and ‘regulatory tax’, and resource availability. The project economics of individual developments are then explored with respect to construction costs. The development, design and construction challenges that impact on cost are also discussed. Furthermore, a breakdown of costs is given in the form of an indicative cost model (for a central London landmark tower) from cost data collected by Davis Langdon in the course of their work as construction consultants. Copyright © 2007 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

t: Bahrain World Trade Center (BWTC): The First Large-Scale Integration of Wind Turbines in a Building
l2: Richard F.  Smith & Shaun Killa, W. S. Atkins & Partners Overseas
d: This paper describes the design evolution of the large-scale wind turbines proposed for the Bahrain Trade Center. It will describe the details of the wind turbines and their control, showing how several innovative ideas have come together and have been technically validated to produce the design for this unique building.

t: Designing the Eco Skyscraper: Premises for Tall Building Design
l2: Ken Yeang, TR Hamzah & Yeang
d: Designing the ecoskyscraper involves configuring its built form and operational systems so that they integrate with nature in a benign and seamless way over its life cycle, by imitating the structure, processes and properties of ecosystems, an approach referred to here as ecomimesis.

t: Sculpted High-rise The Al Hamra Tower
l2: Mark Sarkisian, Neville Mathias,  Aaron Mazeika & et al, SOM
d: At 412m tall on completion, the Al Hamra Tower is set to be amongst the tallest buildings in the world. Setting it apart from other super high-rise buildings is its unique sculpted form.

t: The Sky Court - A Viable Alternative Civic Space for the 21st Century?
l2: Jason Pomeroy, Broadway Malyan
d: This paper puts forward an argument for sky courts as a viable alternative space as an accompaniment, rather than replacement, to the traditional street and square.

t: Burj Dubai Stack Effect - Passive Stack Effect Mitigation Measures in the Design of the World's Tallest Building
l2: Peter A. Weismantle & Luke Leung, SOM
d: The purpose of this paper is to outline the challenges related to stack effect faced by the Burj Dubai design team and to describe the measures taken to mitigate them. The writer hastens to add that this process is still ongoing and will continue through construction and into the operational life of this supertall, unique structure.

t: Maglev Goes High Rise? The Potential of Horizontal Maglev Technology for Vertical High-Rise Elevators
l2: Antony Wood & Corey Baitz, CTBUH
d: Within the past ten years magnetic levitation (maglev) technology has gained substantial interest and significant development internationally. This development has opened up a number of opportunities with regard to elevator system design, elevator travel, and ultimately, high-rise building design.

t: BIM - A Search for Answers
l2: Zak Kostura, ARUP
d: "It’s funny how abruptly industries can change." Zak Kostura writes on how Building Information Modeling changes at an unbelievable rate and the Contemplation on the future of multidisciplinary building design.

t: Structural Design Innovation: Russia Tower
l2: Robert Halvorson & Carrie Warner, Halvorson & Partners
d: The Russia Tower will be Europe’s tallest building and one of the most distinctive high rises in the world. The striking 600 m (1968 ft) tall form and its structure evolved through a collaborative process between the architect, Foster + Partners of London, and structural engineer Halvorson & Partners of Chicago.

t: Sustainability: A New High-Rise Vernacular
l2: Antony Wood, CTBUH
d: This paper outlines the viability of the tall building as a sustainable element in our future urban centres and examines several tall building case studies that have embraced environmental approaches to a lesser or greater degree.

t: The Damped Outrigger Concept for Tall Buildings
l2: Rob J. Smith & Michael R. Willford, ARUP
d: This paper describes new concepts for the structural design of high-rise buildings, in which a system is introduced to increase the dependable structural damping by a factor of 5–10. By so doing the dynamic response of the building to wind effects (buffeting and vortex shedding) is virtually eliminated, leading to substantially reduced lateral design forces and assured occupant comfort.

t: Burj Dubai: An Architectural Technical Design Case Study
l2: Peter A. Weismantle, Gregory L. Smith & Mohamed Sheriff, SOM
d: Beyond the aesthetics of the architectural design, the realization of Burj Dubai is very much a technical design effort. Every system in the Tower must be optimized because of the physical and economic constraints of a super high rise building.

t: Dynamic Behavior of Tall Buildings Under Wind: Insights from Full-Scale Monitoring
l2: Tracy Kijewski-Correa & J. David Pirnia, University of Notre Dame
d: The study highlights the effect of this phenomenon on damping values, the overestimates that can result due to amplitude dependence, as well as the comparatively larger degree of energy dissipation experienced by buildings dominated by frame action.

t: Advances in the Structural Design of High-Rise Residential Buildings in Australia
l2: Owen Martin, Connell Mott MacDonald
d: This paper examines developments in the structural design of high rise concrete residential buildings in Australia’s two major cities, Sydney and Melbourne. Reference is made to four projects where the use of reinforced and post tensioned outriggers in various configurations has been successfully implemented. The 90 storey Eureka Tower project, soon to be completed incorporates a liquid mass damper.

t: Toward Energy Efficient Buildings Worldwide
l2: Peter Warburton & Zak Kostura, Arup
d: Peter Warburton and Zak Kostura of Arup investigate the roadblocks that prevent comprehensive monitoring and publication of building energy performance metrics worldwide, and regional efforts to achieve universal assessment and certification of building consumption levels.

t: Sustainable Design in High-Rise Residential
l2: Dan Kaplan, FX Fowle Architects, PC
d: Major cities and metropolitan areas across the United States have been witnessing a renaissance in urban development. According to the report, Emerging Trends in Real Estate 2006, issued by the Urban Land Institute and PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP last November, one of the current development patterns is a steady demand for urban projects due to continuous downtown migration by empty nesters and young professionals.

t: Cost of Green Revisited: Reexamining the Feasability and Cost Impact of Sustainable Design in the Light of Increased Market Adoption
l2: Peter Morris & Lisa Fay Matthiessen, Davis Langdon
d: The purpose of this paper is to revisit the question of the cost of incorporating sustainable design features into projects. It builds on the work undertaken in the earlier paper “Costing Green: A Comprehensive Cost Database and Budget Methodology,” released in 2004, and looks at the developments that have occurred over the past three years, as sustainable design has become more widely accepted and used.

t: Alternative Forms of Tall Building Evacuation
l2: Antony Wood, CTBUH Executive Director
d: The terrorist attacks on, and consequential collapse of, the World Trade Centre towers on September 11th 2001 has resulted in, arguably, the largest single retrospective analysis of the design of tall buildings since the birth of the typology in the latter stages of the nineteenth century. All aspects of tall building design – safety systems, structure, façade materials, positioning, layout etc – have been called into question, and significant research has – and continues to be – undertaken in the quest to validate and improve the viability of the high rise.

t: Implementing the Paradigm Shift to Use of Elevators/Lifts for Occupant Evacuation in Building Fires
l2: Jake Pauls, Jake Pauls Consulting Services; Daniel A. Johnson, Daniel A. Johnson, Inc; E. Juillet, Life Safety for People with Disabilities, VA
d: A major change, perhaps a revolution or paradigm shift, is coming in the way we will design, manage and use buildings during emergencies that require evacuation of some or all occupants. The change focuses on the use of elevators/lifts in conjunction with, perhaps in preference to, stairs.

t: Learning through Collaboration: an Industry / School of Architecture Partnership
l1: The Journal of the Centre for Education in the Built Environment 2007 Volume 4
l2: Antony Wood & Darran Oxley, University of Nottingham
d: In the academic year 2003/04 a collaborative industry/university project to design an exhibition stand was conducted between the Institute of Architecture at the University of Nottingham and Nottingham-based Canal Engineering.

t: Tallest Buildings Completed in 2006
d: Nina Tower 1 in Hong Kong, at 319 meters high, leads the list of the ten tallest buildings completed in 2006. The Sports City Tower/Aspire Tower (Doha, United Arab Emirates) at 300 meters, and Eureka Tower (Melbourne, Australia), at 296 meters, were second and third, respectively.

t: Performance of Post-Tensioned Slab-Core Wall Connections
l2: Ron Klemencic & J. Andrew Fry, MKA; Gabriel Hurtado & Jack P. Moehle, University of California Berkeley
d: The use of post-tensioned floor slabs and reinforced concrete core walls has become increasingly popular in highrise construction. Questions have arisen concerning the performance of this connection when subjected to gravity loads combined with slab-wall rotations consistent with deformation compatibility of building lateral drifts.

t: High Rise and Genius Loci
l2: Chris Abel, Architectural Writer & Teacher
d: Beginning in the 1980s, tall buildings represent a growing and increasingly important part of the Foster oeuvre, including many of the studio’s iconic designs.

t: Double-deck destination control system
l2: Janne Sorsa & Marja-Liisa Siikonen, KONE
d: Double-deck elevators are used in tall buildings to reduce the core space occupied by elevators. In this article, a destination control system for double-deck elevators is presented and its performance is compared to conventional double-deck elevators as well as to single-deck elevators.

t: Demystifying Construction; Technology in Architectural Education
l1: Journal of Architectural Engineering & Design Management 2006 Volume 2
l2: Antony Wood, University of Nottingham
d: Construction is often seen, among both students and staff at schools of architecture, as a distinct subject taught separately from design. This paper presents an alternative approach to the teaching of construction in schools of architecture.

t: CTBUH Recommendations for the Seismic Design of High-Rise Buildings
l2: Michael Willford, CTBUH
d: The design of tall buildings in seismically active regions varies dramatically from region to region.The objective of this Guide is to set out best-practice principles for the seismic design of high rise buildings for any level of seismic hazard.

t: Jin Mao Tower’s Influence on China’s New Innovative Tall Buildings
l1: 2006 International Seminar of Design and Construction Technologies of Super High-Rise Buildings
l2: Mark Sarkisian, Neville Mathias, Eric Long & Aaron Mazeika, SOM
d: The 56 story, 232 m tall Jinao Tower in Nanjing, China, is a next-generation tower which maximizes performance, efficiency, and occupant experience. Its faceted form is derived from the juxtaposition of an innovative double-skin façade and an external lateral braced steel frame that wraps the tower from crown to base and defines the dimensions.

t: State of the Art Review of Wind Tunnels and Physical Modelling to Obtain Structural Loads and Cladding Pressures
l2: Leighton Cochran, Cermak Peterka Peterson, Inc.
d: The use of the wind tunnel as a building design tool has become much more commonplace in recent years. Its effective use requires close coordination between the architect, the structural engineer and the wind engineer, with the architect traditionally serving as project leader.

t: Evacuation Mode for Total Building Evacuation
l2: Kim Bärlund, Ari Kattainen, Marjukka Mäkelä & Marja-Liisa Siikonen, KONE Corporation
d: The logic of a total evacuation mode of an elevator system includes user interfaces and system interface requirements during an emergency situation. High-level requirements for conventional group control and destination control systems are also significant issues.

t: Culture and Personality: The right place and face for Tall Buildings
l2: Antony Wood, University of Nottingham
d: Tall Buildings are perhaps the most keenly debated building typology currently in existence. This paper presents alternative visions for tall building design.

t: Mixed-Use High Rise in the UK: An Urban Renaissance?
l1: 6th International Conference on Tall Buildings, Hong Kong 2005
l2: Antony Wood, CTBUH Executive Director
d: In many cities around the world, inner-city living is seen as a vital factor in the success of an urban centre. In recent decades in the UK, however, British cities have seen a migration of inhabitants away from the city to the suburban periphery.

t: The Wind Engineering of the Burj Dubai Tower
l2: Peter Irwin, RWDI
d: The Burj Dubai tower will be the world’s tallest building by a wide margin when completed.  Wind is the dominant lateral load and thus governed many aspects of the architectural and structural design.  In order to optimize the design for wind an iterative sequence of wind tunnel tests and design progressions took place in which results from the wind tunnel tests were fed into each step of the design and vice versa.

t: The Shortfall of Tall: The Rise of an Environmental Consciousness in Tall Building Design
l2: Antony Wood, University of Nottingham
d: This presentation will outline the development of this environmental consciousness in high-rise architecture, including some of the experimental work undertaken by the presenter in conjunction with design-research students.

t: Bridging the gap: An analysis of proposed evacuation links at height in the World Trade Centre design competition entries
l2: Antony Wood & Philip Oldfield, University of Nottingham
d: This paper analyses the skybridge links of the numerous re-design proposals for the World Trade Centre Towers, with the aim of establishing their advantages and how they could be incorporated into tall building design in the future.

t: Structural Systems for Tall Apartment Towers
l2: Joseph P. Colaco, CBM Engineers, Inc.
d: Since 1965 there has been a systematic development of structural systems for tall Office Buildings. The current trend is the development of tall Apartment or Mixed - Use buildings.

t: Full-Scale Validation of Finite Element Models for Tall Buildings
l2: William F. Baker, SOM
d: This paper overviews the monitoring program and then specifically focus on the finite element modeling of each of the buildings in the study. The paper will then present a sampling of the periods and damping ratios observed in full-scale under a specific wind event and compare those to the values predicted/assumed in the design stage, with some discussion of the underlying reasons for discrepancy between design predictions/assumptions and full-scale observations.

t: The Effect of Tall Building Base Opening Geometry on Wind Loads and Local Flow Structures
l2: Seifu A. Bekele, Vipac Engineers & Scientists
d: This study focuses on the relation between the shape and size of openings through the base of a tall building, wind loads, and local flow structures. A high frequency force balance will be employed on a 656- foot (200-meter) tall building in a model scale of 1:400 to determine base moments and torsion. The flow structure around the building is investigated using smoke flow visualization and sand scour techniques. The tests are conducted in a detailed modeled immediate surrounding built up surroundings placed in VIPAC boundary layer wind tunnel laboratory.

t: Manhattan’s Mixed Construction Skyscrapers with Tuned Liquid and Mass
l2: Akbar Tamboli, Thornton-Tomasetti Group
d: This paper presents important details, layout, design considerations, commissioning, operation guidelines and benefits of the Tuned Liquid Column Dampers (TLCDs), water-filled custom-shaped tanks, used at Random House Tower. These details can provide other engineers with insights on making use of TLCDs on their high-rise projects. The dynamics and practical functional aspects of TLCDs are presented. Structural response with and without a TLCD is compared. Studies show that a reduction in the maximum wind response of up to 40% can be achieved by using a TLCD.

t: Defensive Design of Federal Office Buildings
l2: Eve Hinman, Hinman Consulting Engineers
d: This paper provides the designer with the tools needed to enhance the life-safety of urban office buildings through practical cost-effective means consistent with the federal anti-terrorist design criteria. It provides an understanding of how explosive loads act on buildings and how contaminants enter into a building, to enable the designer to integrate security needs into the design elegantly through optimization of the site design, architectural design, exterior envelope design, internal adjacencies to create a series of security rings. Case studies of federal buildings using anti-terrorist design principles are presented to illustrate concepts.

t: Aeroelastic Wind-Tunnel Testing Technique Revisited
l2: Risto Kiviluoma, WSP WInd Engineering
d: Objectives of this paper are to address some reasons why and when aeroelastic wind-tunnel testing technique is worth using; propose a new construction principle of scale models borrowed from bridge engineering; and give examples how automated manufacturing could be utilized to speed up testing and reduce design costs. The paper highlights two specific issues, wake buffeting and wind-induced fatigue, which the renewing and redevelopment of urban landscape may bring into wind-resistant design of structures. Tallinn Tornimäe high-rise building, comprising dynamically and aerodynamically coupled twin towers, is used as a demonstrator. This building will the tallest in Estonia, and is currently under construction. It is located in redeveloping city centre comprising five existing high-rise buildings. Another dealt case study is completed new roof structure of the Helsinki Olympic Stadium, Finland, in which the architecture plays important role due to demanding old environment.

t: Bridging the Gap: Proposed Evacuation Links at Height in the World Trade Center Design Entries
l2: Antony Wood & Philip Oldfield, University of Nottingham
d: This paper proposes to analyse in detail the skybridge links of the WTC replacement designs – impact on evacuation, structure, façade, tower operation etc – with the aim of establishing the advantages of such linkages, and how these could be incorporated into tall building design in the future.

t: Mass Transit: The Key to Urban Development, Urban Renewal, and Sustainable Cities
l2: Porie Saikia-Eapen, MTA-NYC Transit; Robert E. Paaswell, City College of New York
d: This paper argues mass transit is the key to urban development, urban renewal and sustainable cities with New York City as the case study.

t: Linking Means of Egress Systems and Emergency Response Strategies
l2: Jake Pauls, Jake Pauls Consulting Services
d:
The prime objective of the paper will be to demonstrate key functional links between design of means of egress (including more-or-less traditional exits and non-conventional elevators) and urban design incorporating tall buildings.

A multifaceted record of international research and technological developments will be presented, beginning in 1967 and paralleling the life and aftermath of New York’s World Trade Center. Human behavior in fires and other emergencies, broadly-based concerns for life safety, and usability will be subsequently addressed in contexts of architecture, engineering, ergonomics (human factors), building codes/standards, and public health.

t: High-Rise Buildings with Twisted Façades
l2: Karel J. Vollers, Free-D Geometries
d: Increasingly high-rise buildings feature twisted façades. Often such buildings are intended to become icons and give identity to major urban schemes and the cities where they are (to be) built. Many well known architects made spectacular plans. Initially classed as visionary, the projects since 1990 increasingly appeared in competitions and feasibility studies all over the world. Now the first have been built. The author classifies buildings shapes with twisted surfaces and illustrates their usage in high-rise. Various aspects are elaborated on: geometric manipulations, visual aspects and the increasing interest in the use of façade diagrids.

t: Urban Development in Asia
l2: Stephan S. Huh, Parker Durrant International
d:
Busan is the second largest city in Korea, in constant competition against Seoul for the title of largest. Some of its efforts to rejuvenate part of city have included proposing the tallest building. This discussion will cover the existing city fabric and how it is affecting the new development of this super-tall tower in an effort to generate other follow-up development in the city.

How much super-tall buildings benefit other buildings that may follow will be discussed. Pusan is planning to build this memorable super-tall building to attract not only the people of Korea but also those from all over the world. A new monorail system will connect the city and connect the Pusan bullet train station through Lotte Tower to Taejong Dae, a top tourist spot. The presentation will also discuss a 50-story tower in Seoul and 54-story tower in Hochiminh City in Vietnam.

t: Three Mega-Tall Buildings in Hong Kong: Central Plaza, Two International Finance Centre, and International Commerce Centre
l2: John W. K. Luk, Julia M.K. Lau & Tim M.T. Mak; Lau Sun Hung Kai Properties
d: The paper goes over Sun Hung Kai Properties Group as the developer/co-developer of three major tall building commercial complexes in Hong Kong: the Central Plaza, the International Finance Centre and the Union Square and also discuss the impacts or influences of these developments to Hong Kong and its nearby cities, particularly those on the mainland of China.

t: Amsterdam’s Zuidas: A New Urban Landscape
l2: Pi di Bruijn, de Architekten Cie
d:
Globalization and rising mobility are causing significant changes of emphasis in the Dutch urban landscape. The individual cities of Amsterdam, Rotterdam, The Hague, and Utrecht are increasingly merging into an urban network that is frequently referred to as the “Deltametropolis.” A good situation, excellent connections, tight communications, favorable climate, well-educated workforce, and international orientation furnish the Deltametropolis a powerful position for global activity.

This paper focuses on the potential of the Zuidas to rise above the local scale, as it can supplement business activity on the supranational level. The Zuidas can be a metaphor of modern society, an open society founded on local values, and one that can stand its ground in a global context, offering opportunities to play a vital role in this world.

t: Experimental Tall Structures and Natural Energy Systems
l2: Peter Land, Illinois Institute of Technology
d:
New challenges in energy require unique solutions in structures, and fresh ideas in structures and materials generate new concepts and solutions. Consequently, the areas of structure, energy, and materials are becoming closely intertwined; and advances in these areas influence progress in tall building design and technology.

This paper will show a number of tall building projects with innovative structures and energy systems selected from a range of proposals developed at the Illinois Institute of Technology by the author with advanced students over several years. They include concepts such as multiple co-supportive tubes, geometries informed by nano-tube lattices, exo-skeleton and global tensegrity, shell-action diagrids, lightpipe penetration, etc.

t: Innovative Tall Building Form Development
l2: Sang Min Park, Illinois Institute of Technology
d:
This paper will propose a series of methods to develop concepts and options of tall building form to the architect and engineer when designing geometrically complex buildings during schematic design.

The intention of the research on this topic is to inspire more interest in developing an innovative approach to the design of tall building forms through the integration of architecture and digital technology. The focus of this presentation is to suggest the generative forms and digital methods as applicable to architecture by means of architectural evaluations and efficient process in designing tall buildings. The suggested forms and the digital design methods presented will be of great interest in the tall building design field.

t: Sequential Analysis of Flat-Slab Construction and Its Impact on Construction Cycle
l2: Seung-Chang Lee, Jae-Yo Kim, Seon-Chong Gang, Kyung-Yuel Choi & Ahmad Abdelrazaq, Samsung Engineering & Construction
d: This paper will study the behavior of flat-plate slab system behavior when utilizing HPC during construction and its impact on long-term performance.

t: Tall Buildings in the Urban Context of Dhaka City
l2: Asif Iqbal, Bangladesh Consultants
d: The history and present status of development of tall buildings in Dhaka City is presented in the background of its evolution and growth. It is observed that the number of tall buildings has increased very fast  in the last two decades. But there haven’t been sufficient improvements in the infrastructure necessary to provide services to these buildings.The past and present trends of urbanization in Bangladesh characterize the growth of Dhaka as typical ‘dependent urbanization’ in third world countries. In this situation, when it is expected that Dhaka will be one of the top 10 cities in the world in respect of population in the next 25 years, appropriate strategies have to be adopted to make the city more habitable.

t: Prediction Method for Response of an Elasto-Plastic Structure to Fluctuating Wind Force Based on Energy Balance
l2: Keisuke Yoshie, Nikken Sekkei; Haruyuki Kitamura, Tokyo University of Science; Takeshi Ohkuma, Kanagawa University
d: The purpose of this paper is to show a prediction method of elasto-plastic response under fluctuating wind forces based on energy balance.

t: Financial Aspects That Drive Design Innovations for Tall Buildings
l2: Robert M. Lau, Roosevelt University
d: This paper will present economic issues that drove design solutions for Tall Buildings. Starting from the 19th century planning of Central Business Districts (rectilinear), we will address how investors and end users received value as ‘more for the same cost’ that made these projects financial successes. If financiers see good investment values, they will direct the designers to incorporate them into the project.

t: The Skyscraper: Epitome of Human Aspirations
l2: Mir M. Ali, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
d: This paper will discuss the underlying human striving towards something higher to conquer space, as well as psychological motivations for designing skyscrapers and not so much their craftsmanship and artistry. The discussion will demonstrate how skyscrapers fit into the changing city fabric all over the world. The conclusion will be drawn that they represent human aspirations and are inseparable from the permeable urban fabric and public life.

t: Estate Renewal Through Development of Prefabricated Super High-Rise Buildings
l2: Chor Cheong Fong, Housing and Development Board of Singapore
d: This paper presents Housing & Development Board (HDB) of Singapore’s solution in the development of prefabricated super high-rise residential buildings to solve Singapore’s land scarce problem, incorporating full precast technology as an efficient and cost effective solution to help renew its urban landscape.

t: Tall Building Emergency Evacuation: “Time To Think Differently”
l2: Dr. Jonathan (Yoni) Shimshoni, Escape Rescue Systems
d: This paper presents developments in the area of high-rise building emergency evacuation solutions, especially in three categories: Platform Devices; Chute Devices; and Controlled Descent Devices, discusses the need for such devices, the applicability and benefits of the different solutions, the approach taken in a number of other countries and insight into the process of US codes and standards.

t: Reflexive Urbanism as a Model for Urban Regeneration
l2: Kai van Hasselt, Shinsekai Analysis
d: This paper call for a business model that is build around the value capturing of externalities through innovative capital pooling and a Public-Private Partnership approach in regenerating our cities in a reflexive manner. This paper will outline possibilities for such an approach, and show examples of transport, cultural and clustering based redevelopment.

t: 126 Phillip Street, Sydney
l2: Rocco Bressi, Bovis Lend Lease
d: 126 Phillip Street is a super premium office tower containing 31 commercial levels, newly completed on the corner of Phillip and Hunter streets in Sydney. With 462,850 square feet (43,000 square meters) of office space, it has commanding and unrestricted views of Sydney Harbor and the Botanical Gardens. It will be recognized by its distinctive architectural roof feature, adding to the city sideline on the eastern edge of the city. 126 Phillip Street presents a new office typology for Australia through the combination of the three key building elements: core, atrium, and floor plate. This presentation will discuss structural aspects of each.

t: Evacuation Mode for Total Building Evacuation
l2: Kim Bärlund, Ari Kattainen, Marjukka Mäkelä & Dr Marja-Liisa Siikonen, KONE
d: This paper discusses functional requirements of elevator group controllers of an emergency evacuation elevator system. The focus is on the necessary logic of a total evacuation mode of the elevator system.

t: Enhancing Safety and Security in High-Rise Building through Sustainable Design
l2: Faridah Shafii, Universiti Teknologi Malaysia
d: This paper will discuss the safety/security and sustainable synergies for the planning and design of high-rise buildings to minimize environmental impacts as well as ensuring the health, safety, security, and comfort of high-rise building occupants. Illustrative examples in existing high-rise buildings will be presented.

t: Fire-Resistant Steel, A New Approach to Structural Safety
l2: Roger Wildt, RW Consulting Group
d: This paper summarizes the development of these steels, testing programs to evaluate structural fire performance, applications for which these steels have been used over the past decade, and comparisons with traditional structural steels. Included are a number of photographs of both applications and testing. The introduction of new steels raises a substantive issue regarding the criteria by which steel assemblies currently are evaluated by fire test standards.

t: Multihazard Design of Tall Buildings
l2: Gregory C. Freeman, Joong C. Lee & Mohammed M. Ettouney, Weidlinger Associates
d: This paper presents several applications of multihazard considerations for tall buildings. Three realistic examples are given. The first example explores the interaction of building resiliencies as it resists different hazards (wind and progressive collapse). Another example shows that the efficiencies of different structural systems (EBF vs. CBF) can vary, depending on the types of multihazards considered. Finally, it is shown that when hazards conflict in their demands on the building, the effects on life-cycle costs can be large. In all, the cost implications of including or ignoring multihazards during design phase are shown to be in the range of 1.5%-18%. The proposed methods and examples show that quantifying multihazard design of tall buildings must be performed during the design phase in order to save unnecessary expenditures.

t: New Metropolitan Living and the Skycraper in a European City
l2: Jan Klerks, CTBUH
d: This paper will look at the historic and current European residential skyscraper developments as the embodiment of new metropolitan living. It will address related issues dealing with high-rise zoning, laws and regulation, the definition of urbanity, population typologies based on residential preferences, past and present inner city development, and especially the marketing and branding of metropolitan living, all within a European context.

t: An Aesthetics of Sustainability
l2: Lawrence S. Ng, Cesar Pelli & Associates
d: Cesar Pelli recently completed the design of two tall buildings: one in Madrid and one in the United Arab Emirates, both extreme climatic conditions. Both projects have to reconcile the desire for transparency with the demand for physical comfort and concerns for energy.

t: Two Examples of Urban Renewal: Melbourne and Coolangatta, Australia
l2: Omiros Emmanouilides, Omiros One Architecture
d: The proposed paper will examine and compare two recent and totally different examples of urban renewal in these two Australian cities. The area to be examined in Melbourne is the recently completed Docklands area, and in Coolangatta the new foreshore redevelopment on Marine parade and Griffith streets.

t: Sustainable Design: Daylighting Study of Kuwait Autism Center
l2: Abdullah Al-Mohaisen & Omar Khattab, Kuwait University
d: This paper discusses a case study of the architectural design of Kuwait Autism Centre that adopted, as a major design definer, the provision of adequately lit educational spaces for autistic people.

t: The Skybridge as an Evacuation Option for Tall Buildings in High-Rise Cities in the Far East
l1: Journal of Applied Fire Science Vol 13 Issue II
l2: Antony Wood, University of Nottingham; Wan-ki Chow, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University; Dean McGrail, WSP Hong Kong Ltd.
d: Consequent to the World Trade Center (WTC) tragedy, research into the safety of tall buildings has been concentrated almost exclusively on the improvement of structural systems, fire proofing, and vertical evacuation systems. While this work is vital toward making tall buildings safer, it is in itself not enough.

t: Wind Engineering for the Las Vegas Stratosphere Tower
l2: Daryl W. Boggs & Roy Denoon, Cermak Peterka Peterson, Inc; Brent Wright, Wright Engineers
d: A wind tunnel model of the Las Vegas Stratosphere Tower was first tested as part of its design in 1991, but this proved to be only the beginning of a long series of modifications and additions that warranted additional wind-engineering investigations lasting through 2004.

t: Elevator selection with destination control system
l2: Janne Sorsa, Henri Hakonen & Marja-Liisa Siikonen, KONE
d: Elevator planning is based on up-peak and calculation of handling capacity to meet the building traffic flow demand. In this article, a method to select the elevators with a destination control system is introduced so that the service level will be good in all traffic situations.

t: Too Little, Too Late?
l2: Chris Abel, University of Sydney
d: Chris Abel writes for the Architectural Review Australia on the idealization to the detached, single-family home.

t: Hybrid Structures in Challenging Architecture
l2: J. Vambersky, Delft University of Technology
d: This paper will consider the concepts, alternatives, final solutions, and details of the two architecturally appealing buildings, ‘Malietoren’ in the Netherlands and the ‘Belvedere’ in Rotterdam,  in illustrating the hybrid development.

t: Rising High in Manhattan, Trump World Tower, The Tallest Residential Building in the World
l2: Ahmad Rahimian, WSP Cantor Seinuk; Kenneth A. Hiller, Bovis Lend Lease
d: This paper presents the pioneering colutions in the design and constructuon of the Trump World Tower.

t: Deformation Capacity of Composite Beam Determined by Rupture
l2: Satoshi Yamada & Ken Okada, Tokyo Institute of Technology; Sang-Hoon Oh, Research Institute of Industrial Science and Technology
d: In the Hyogoken-Nanbu Earthquake (Kobe Earthquake), damage of rupture in the beam-to-column connection was mainly observed in steel building structures. After the earthquake, a lot of experimental research works on the deformation capacity of steel beam, which is determined by rupture, were carried out. From those research works, the cause of the rupture, such as stress concentration at connection part and the lack of the quality of steel material, was pointed out.

t: Retrofit of Existing Steel Moment Connections with Floor Slabs
l2: Sang-Hoon Oh, RIST; Young-Ju Kim & Tae-Sup Moon, Hanyang University
d: The effectiveness of retrofit methods on the deformation capacity of existing steel moment connections with concrete slabs were investigated. Five full-scale beam-to-column subassemblages were tested. Tests included one conventional specimen and four retrofit specimens. Three types of retrofit methods for composite beam connections introduced in detail: RRS, RBS & RSS details. These retrofit methods applied to the bottom flange of beams only.

t: Stress Measuring of Structural Members using Long Gauge Fiber Optic Sensors
l2: Jae Min Baek, Hyun Seok Jung & Hyo Seon Park, Yonsei University
d: Fiber optic sensor has been considered as one of the major sensors to monitor structural behavior due to its durability and independence on EMI. Most existing fiber optic sensors used for health monitoring systems, however, can cover a relatively small range of structural members. In this case, the reliability of the evaluated safety depends on the number and location of the point sensors. In this paper a new health monitoring technique based on average strains from long gauge sensors is suggested. The mathematical models to transform the measured average strains from LGFOS into the maximum strain in structural members are developed.

t: Modal Identification and Model Updating for Better Controlled Structures
l2: Soon Ho Cho, Gwangju University
d: Recent technologies on the modal identification and model updating procedures related to ambient vibration testing, the corresponding output-only modal analysis and the integration of test and analysis results for large civil engineering structures are reviewed.

t: Preliminary Experimental Test for the Behavior of Steel Plate Shear Walls in Super High-Rise Building Structures
l2: Wonki Kim, Jaeseok Lee & Sangsu Park, Hoseo University
d: -In this paper steel plate shear wall is studied to resist the lateral loads instead of reinforced concrete wall system. But, there are lacks of research and understanding of steel plate shear wall system so that no real building of such system exists in Korea. This paper demonstrates stability and serviceability of steel plate shear wall by preliminary experimental test.

t: New Ductile Steel Frame Limiting Damage to the Connection Elements at the Bottom Flange of Beam-Ends
l2: Shoichi Kishiki, Satoshi Yamada & Toru Takeuchi, Tokyo Institute of Technology; Kazuaki Suzuki & Akira Wada, Nippon Steel Corporation
d: The purpose of this study is to propose new ductile steel frame, which realizes not only structural performances but also easy repairing after an earthquake. In the proposed system, beams are bolted to columns with T-stubs, and plastic deformation, which is damage, is limited to the T-stubs at the bottom flange of beam-ends.

t: A Framework for Scheduling in High-rise Building Construction
l2: Hyun-Soo Lee, Jung-Ho Yu & Sang-Hoon Song, Seoul National University
d: The main purpose of this study is to propose a framework for scheduling a high-rise building construction. The framework views the scheduling process as a series of decision-making steps. Thus, it consists of relevant decision-making areas, associated impact factors and interrelationship among the factors.

t: Design Challenges of Tall Buildings and Implications on Construction Education
l2: Faridah Shafii & M. Zahry Othman, Universiti Teknologi Malaysia
d: This paper reviews and discuss the design considerations for enhancing performance in future tall buildings in the context of sustainability and safety/security. Their impacts upon construction and design educations were discussed.

t: Use of Super Elements for An Efficient Analysis of High-rise Building Structures
l2: Dong-Guen Lee, Taeho Kim & Dong-Kon Lee, Sunkyunkwan University
d: This paper presents an efficient model for the analysis of a high-rise building structure using super elements. Super elements for a floor slab and shear wall are introduced and static and dynamic analysis of example structures were performed to demonstrate the accuracy and efficiency of the proposed method.

t: Managing Mixed-use Housings within Urban Design District in Seoul
l2: Heeji Lim, Seoul Development Institute
d: This study examines why high-rise mixed-use housings have been developed excessively in the urban design districts and proposes ways to control the excessive development and increase publicity of the high-rise mixed-use housings.

t: Elements of the Tall Building 2004
l2: Brian Douglas Lee, SOM
d: " The design of tall buildings has always centered about desires for maximizing development rights, individual and corporate ego, and civic pride." Brian Douglas Lee of SOM writes on the elements that makes a tall building in 2004.

t: Public Space Planning of Mixed-use High-rise Buildings - Focusing on the Use and Impact of Deck Structure in an Urban Development in Seoul
l2: Eun-Ju Park & Jae-Hyun Shim, Sejong University; Seung Park, Samoo
d: The purpose of this study is to analyze the planning and the design of public space at the street level of Mixed-use tall buildings currently developed and constructed in Korea. In recent years, Deck structure is highly used in many large scale projects in term of separating and providing safe pedestrian access from the vehicles.

t: Structural Quantity Analysis of Tall Buildings
l2: Han-Wook Cho, Song-Geun Roh & Young-Min Byun, HanmiParsons Co., Ltd; Kyong-Soo Yom, Harmony Engineering Co.
d: Structural construction costs of tall buildings recently built or under construction in Korea were investigated in terms of unit material quantities and their costs. The itemized investigation of structural construction cost gives a general view for overall construction cost composition as well as the significance of selected structural members and their materials in the cost of constructing high rise buildings.

t: A Study of Carrying Historical Monuments without Dismantling into Pieces
l2: Ipek Nese Sener & Ali Ihsan Unay, Middle East Technical University
d: In this paper, finite element models and transportation as-a-whole projects are discussed for certain minarets that are currently located in the South Anatolian town of Hasankeyf, which is threatened by the rising waters of a nearby dam.

t: Highrise – Lowland
l2: Pi de Bruijn, de Architekten Cie
d: In order to discover the origins of the almost emotional resistance to high-rise construction and why attitudes have changed, we shall first examine the physical conditions and the socio-economic context of the Netherlands. We shall subsequently compare the framework of high-rise construction in two contrasting cities: the world port of Rotterdam, which lost its historical heart during a bombardment in the Second World War, and the capital Amsterdam, whose historical centre as a whole has the status of a monument.

t: A New Process to Improve Tall Buildings Heat Resistance and Security
l2: B. Bastita, Free-Lance Researcher
d: The concept is a combination of load-bearing metal frame and fire-fighting system. The principle is based on the use of the metal frame as the spraying liquid supply to the sprinklers. In this way, the system ensures, at the same time during a blaze, the liquid supply of the fire-fighting system and the cooling of the frame that keeps its mechanical properties.

t: FEM Analysis on Structural Behavior of CFT Column to Flat Plate Slab Connection
l2: Young K. Ju, University of Texas at Austin;  Yong-Chul Kim & Sang-Dae Kim, Korea University
d: Since 1990s, the construction of tall buildings has been greatly increased. For the tall buildings, the story height is an important factor to accomplish economic buildings. Many researchers including the authors have studied the structural systems reducing a story height of the buildings.

t: Dynamic Properties of Tall Buildings in Korea
l2: Sung-Won Yoon, Seoul National University of Technology; Young-Kyu Ju, University of Texas at Austin
d: This paper is concerned with the natural frequency and damping ratios of steel and reinforced concrete buildings. It describes the vibration measurement methods employed for testing buildings and presents reliable methods of assessing the natural period and damping ratio.

t: Aeroelastic Responses of Tall Building to Wind Loads using TLD
l2: Young-Moon Kim & Ki-Pyo You, Chonbuk National University; Hee-Jung Ham, Kwangwon National University
d: In this paper, passive TLD installed on the top of the aeroelastic building model of which have different side ratio of 2 and 3 and aspect ratio of 8, was used for wind tunnel experiment.

t: Control of Wind-Induced Acceleration Response of 46-Story R.C. Building Structure
l2: Sang Kyung Ahn & Jung- Gun Oh, Samsung Engineering & Construction; Kyung-Won Min &  Ji-Hun Park, Dankook University et al
d: The purpose of this study is to evaluate the performance of viscoelastic dampers (VED) practically used for enhancing the serviceability of a 46-story reinforced concrete residential building structure. Considering that bracing system for the installation of VED is not appropriate for residential building because it occupies large interior space, a method for installing VED at midspan of horizontal beam connecting the core and exterior columns is proposed.

t: Structural Design of SK Euljiro Headquaters
l2: Jidong Kim, Jinkyong Kim & Jongho Kim, Chang & Minwoo Structural Consultant
d: This building planning to be used for SK-Telecom headquarters has 36 stories (include roof floors) above and 6 stories below grade, and the building features uniquely shaped exterior of two sloping facade on the south side of the tower, as well as the top portion of the north side sloping.

t: High Flown Architecture and Ascendancy of the Building
l2: Alfred B. Hwangbo, Seoul National University of Technology
d: This paper intends to assert, from particular frame of reference in architectural history and theory, that more careful building strategies are needed for a better balanced development in future, as we learn an important social implication from the recent high-rise building projects in Seoul.

t: Urbanization Characteristic of the Skyscraper
l2: Huang Shiyan, Dalian University
d: The development of economy at full speed has accelerated the paces of the urbanization process. Rural surplus population is centralized to the city producing the demand for the urban land. In order to offer the usable floor area to the maximum extent on the limited land, under the support of new material, new craft and intelligent technology-

t: Seismic Performance of RC Frame Retrofitted by Steel Plate Slit Damper
l2: Waon-Ho Yi, Won-Jik Yang, Dae-Eon Kang & Jung-Han Lee, Kwangwoon University; Sang-Hoon Oh, Research Institute of Industrial Science and Technology
d: The objectives of this study are 1) to find out the shear behavior and the shear capacity of RC bare frames, and damper-retrofitted RC frames, 2) to evaluate the equation of the shear capacity of RC frames by comparing the Japanese Standard equations and Modified equations adopting the test results for the seismic performance evaluation of low rise RC structures in Korea, 3) to analyze and compare the hysteretic behavior under the lateral loads using the analysis models such as the D-Tri model and the Bi-Linear model.

t: Potential of Vertical Forms: Learning Sustainability from Yemeni Tower Architecture
l2: Khaled A. Al-Sallal, UAE University
d: Extreme transformations have taken place in the architecture of Yemen during the last forty years as a result of several important political, economic, and social forces. The vertical forms that gave Yemeni architecture its unique identity have transformed into horizontal forms that have no roots or identity.

t: Structural Design of Base-Isolation system for Tall Building in Japan
l2: Yozo Shinozaki, Osamu Hosozawa & Tsutomu Komuro, Taisei Corporation
d: The high-rise buildings with the base isolation system have been realized by studying the practical applicability of this system, that is, by extracting and resolving the several problems in the actual design. Sendai MT building is the first base-isolated building with over 60 m height in Japan, and Thousand Tower was the tallest base-isolated residential Tower in Japan when completed.

t: A Study on Pendulum Seismic Isolators for High-Rise Buildings
l2: Ikuo Tatemichi, Maeda Corp; Mamoru Kawaguchi, Kawaguchi & Engineers; Masaru Abe, Hosei Unviersity
d: This paper presents an experimental study on the effectiveness of the non-parallel swing system and on its applicability to high-rise structures.

t: Structural Design of the Tallest Concrete Tower in Korea, the # Star City
l2: Hi Sun Choi & Dennis Poon, Thornton Tomasetti; Jongho Kim, Chang & Minwoo Structural Consultant; Seongsoo Kim, Seong-Jin Structural Group
d: Residential high-rise buildings, such as apartments and condominiums, were introduced to provide multiple residential spaces in urban and suburban areas for their economic advantage and convenience for commuting to urban business districts.

t: Structural Analysis and Design of Transferred Shear Wall Type Structure
l2: Jong Soo Kim, Kyoung-Ah Lee, Dong Hwan Kim & Hyung-Seok Park, CS Structural Engineering Inc.
d: The PARKVIEW is a grand scale residential complex in the suburbs of Seoul. That is comprised of 13 buildings with 30~35 stories above ground and 1 basement floor. The 428m

t: A Study on a Proper Reduction Process of Indoor Air Pollutants in Newly-Constructed High-rise Multi-Family Houses
l2: Hyung Kyu Yu, Jin Chul Park & Eon Ku Rhee, Chung-Ang University
d: The purpose of this study is to investigate actual air quality in newly-constructed high-rise multi-family houses and to improve Indoor Air Quality(IAQ). The field measurement was conducted in four different newly-constructed high-rise multi-family house complexes in Seoul and one existing multi-family house complex in Kyong-gi province.

t: Shear Response of Reinforced Concrete Columns with Different Axial Loads and Shear Span-to-depth Ratios
l2: Sang-Woo Kim & Jung-Yoon Lee, Sungkyunkwan University
d: The shearing force seldom acts on its own but rather in combination with axial force and flexure. Thus the interaction between shear, axial force, and flexure has to be taken into account when developing a rational truss model to predict the shear behavior and ensure the safe design of reinforced concrete (RC) members.

t: A Study on the Evaluation Methods of Equivalent Design Wind Loads for Tall Buildings
l2: Won K. Kim, Haejun Park & Jihye Chun, Seoul National University; Sanggon Lee, Risk Management Support, Korea
d: So far, simulation works are mostly performed to estimate the room of fire origin. However, there are not many studies on post fire situation, especially for fire propagation beyond its room of fire origin.

t: Monitoring of Structural Behavior of High-rise Buildings using GPS
l2: Hyo Seon Park, Hong Gyoo Shon, Ill Soo Kim & Jae Hwan Park, Yonsei Unviersity
d: A new displacement measuring system using GPS is introduced for monitoring of the lateral and torsional displacements of high-rise buildings. To develop the system, error ranges of the GPS measurement data are examined by varying the distance between a reference point and measuring points.

t: Optimization of Large-Scale Structures using Hybrid Genetic Algorithms
l2: Yun Han Kwon & Hyo Seon Park, Yonsei University
d: In this paper, parallel and distributed hybrid genetic algorithms are proposed to avoid the cumbersome process related to the parameter setting and to reduce convergence time with small size of population. To demonstrate the performance, the algorithms are implemented on high-performance computers such as IBM Regatta P690 system and a cluster of personal computers.

t: An Analytical Model for High-rise Wall-frame Building Structures
l2: Minsik Bang & Jaehong Lee, Sejong University, Republic of Korea
d: In this paper, the governing equations of a wall-frame building are formulated through the continuum approach and the whole structure is idealized as a shear–flexural cantilever. The effect of shear deformation of the wall and flexural deformation of the frame are considered and incorporated in the formulation of the governing equations.

t: Damping Coefficients for Weak and Moderate Earthquake Ground Motions
l2: Myeong-Han Kim, Kyungwoon University; Myoung-Ho Oh, Hyundai Institute of Construction Technology; Young K. Ju, University of Texas at Austin; Sang-Dae Kim, Korea University
d:
In this study, damping coefficients are based on the weak and moderate ground motions are developed and compared to those of current
seismic design code provisions.

t: HOBEM: High-rise Office Building Evaluation Model
l2: Gahng-Ju Lee, Changwon National University; Sung-Moon Jung, Samoo Architects & Engineers
d: The purpose of this study is on the establishment of evaluation model for high-rise office building, and its subject is as follows. (1) Theoretical consideration for the building evaluation: concept and characteristic of building evaluation, concept and evaluation factors of building performance. (2) Study for evaluation models of office buildings: main issue and current tendencies of office evaluation, office environmental evaluation system by Vischer and REN(The Real Estate Norm) by Jonge & Gray. (3) Establishment of evaluation model for high-rise office building: this study proposed HOBEM as High-rise Office Building Evaluation Model.

t: A Study on the Optimal Hot-water Distribution of Pipe Network for Radiant Floor Heating in High-rise Residential Buildings
l2: Seong-Ryong Ryu, Seok-Jin Hong, Myoung-Souk Yeo & Kwang-Woo Kim, Seoul University; Ho-Tae Seok, Yeungnam University
d: In this study, we performed measurements and analyses of flow rate of pipe network for radiant floor heating in existing residential buildings through a field survey in order to find out the actual conditions and the existing problems.

t: Evaluation of the Insulation Performance of Curtain Walls in High-rise Residential Buildings
l2: Kang-Soo Kim, Sang-Tae No & Chang-Sung Kim, Korea University
d: There are two main objectives in this research; first, to perform steady state conduction simulations for curtain wall materials in high-rise outdoor conditions, and second, to evaluate insulation efficiency and moisture condensation on curtain wall surfaces based on the results of the simulations.

t: Strategies for Tall Urban Buildings
l2: William Pedersen, KPF
d: Tall buildings are, by their nature, insular and autonomous. The demands of their own biology make them resistant to forming linkages and relationships with other structures.

t: Prediction of Creep and Shrinkage for Column Shortening of High-rise Buildings Using Neural-Network
l2: Waon-Ho Yi, Won-Jik Yang, Dae-Eon Kang & Jung-Han Lee, Kwangwoon University
d: The objectives of this study are to develop and evaluate the Neural Network algorithm which can predict the inelastic shortening such as the creep strain and the drying shrinkage strain of reinforced concrete members using the previous test data.

t: A Study on the High Strength Steel for the Building Structure
l2: Sung-Woo Im, Yosuk Kim & In-Hwa Chang, Steel Structure Research Lab, RIST
d: There is a great need for high strength steel especially for the high-rise steel building structure. High strength steels, however, may have mechanical properties that are significantly different from those of the conventional steels.

t: Introduction to Korea Building Code(Draft)
l2: Bong-Soo Jeon, Jeon and Associates
d: The draft of the Korean Building Code (KBC) is briefly described in this document to enable the reader to study the systems of building codes in Korea. The KBC draft was developed by the Architectural Institute of Korea (AIK) and supported by the Ministry of Construction and Transportation (MOCT) in 2003.

t: Up-down Construction of Reinforced Concrete Tall Building
l2: Somsak Swaddiwudhipong & Seng-Lip Lee,  The National University of Singapore
d: Up-down construction of reinforced concrete tall building is described through the construction of Chareon Krung Tower project, a 70-storey building with 6 levels of basement in downtown Bangkok, Thailand. Top-down basement construction makes use of permanent structural members for excavation support system dispensing the unnecessary cost of temporary works.

t: Natural Ventilation Performance of a Double-skin Façade with a Solar Chimney
l2: Wenting Ding & Yuji Hasemi, Waseda University; Tokiyoshi Yamada, National Research Institute of Fire and Disaster
d: Double-skin façade is getting more and more attention as it provides many possibilities for energy conservation and at the same time creates good indoor environment. In this paper natural ventilation performance of a double-skin façade is highlighted.

t: Evaluation of Stack Effect according to the Shape and the Window Area Ratios of Lobby in High-rise Buildings
l2: Jung-Yeon Yu, Dong-Woo Cho, Ki-hyung Yu & Hae-Kwon Jung, Korea Institute of Construction Technology
d: On this study, computer simulations for airflow analysis according to the shape of lobby and the area ratios of windows to walls of the lobby envelope are performed to develop the architectural plans for stack effect problems in high-rise buildings in cold climates.

t: Overview on Indoor Thermal Environment and Air Quality Issues for Residential Buildings in Japan
l2: Hiroshi Yoshino, Tohoku University
d: This paper deals with energy consumption and the problems of indoor environment found in residential buildings. Especially, the issue of SBS in houses and the current research and development for preventing the SBS will be introduced.

t: Urban Design – Vancouver: Creating High Density Urban Neighbourhoods
l2: James Cheng, James KM Cheng Architects
d: James Cheng speaks on creating high density in urban neighborhoods in Vancouver and the importance to abandon the old planning methods of segregating uses.

t: An Explorative Study for Space Analysis Techniques for Tall and Huge-Scale Buildings
l2: Jaepil Choi, Hyeongkyu Cho & Hyunchul Choi, Seoul National University
d: This paper, in the viewpoint, is to apply a spatial analysis tool to a tall and huge-scale building in order to examine the tool’s reliability to estimate the potential space usage through the comparison between the analysis result and the actual space usage.

t: Space Efficiency in Multi-Use Tall Building
l2: Hyeong-ill Kim & Mahjoub Elnimeiri, Illinois institute of Technology
d: This paper seeks to make a contribution to the development of the design strategies for multi-use tall buildings in relation to space efficiency of the building for architects, engineers & developers during their early phases of the design process.

t: An Experimental Study on the Incombustibility of Polymer Mortar
l2: Kyung-Hee Chae & Man-Sung Hur, Woosong Technical College; Youn-Su Lee, Juseong College
d: This study evaluated the capacity of incombustibility of the polymer mortar according to the contents of flame retarder and binder.

t: Concrete Contribution to Shear Resistance by Different Crack Modeling
l2: Soon Ho Cho, Gwangju University
d: The fixed-angle crack model based modified compression field theory (MCFT) was developed to include the slip deformation across the crack, thereby allowing for the non-coincident directions of the principal strain and stress. To investigate the significance of crack modeling on the shear resistance, a series of tests on beams without transverse reinforcement was predicted by both rotating- and fixed-angle crack models within the frame of the MCFT. The results predicted by the fixed-angle MCFT were comparable to those by the rotating-angle MCFT when the initial crack angle of 45 deg. and the corresponding friction law are adopted.

t: Monitor Structure in Seismic Zones: Structural Health Monitoring
l2: James Stowell, Leica Geosystems Inc.; Tracy Kijewski-Correa & Michael Kochly, Notre Dame University
d: With the exception of tsunamis (earthquake tidal waves) and earthquake-triggered landslides, most earthquake related fatalities are caused by the collapse of people's homes upon them, or from fires that develop after earthquakes. A doubling in world population (6 billion -> 12 billion) is expected in the next 100 years. Half of the world's super-cities and many newly developing mega-cities are located in seismically hazardous locations, Seismic Zones, where new apartment complex starts are at an all-time high.
