d: This paper investigates the performance of individual floor trusses of the type used at the World Trade Center using software developed over a number of years to model the behavior of steel and composite framed structures in fire. A variety of scenarios are considered, including different truss forms, degree of protection, and span length, and load ratio. Issues which are considered include the identification of critical truss members, the interaction between flexural behavior and centenary action at increasing levels of deflection, and the forces which develop in the connections. Results are the use of such structural forms for different fire resistance requirements.

t: Cost Comparison Between North American Codes and British Collapse Measures
l2: Barry Charnish, Halcrow Yolles
d: This paper will provide a review of the specific code clauses an estimate of the structural cost premium, based on a normalized design, with costs provided by Canadian, American, and British Contractors and Sub-contractors. These costs will be further reviewed in comparison with the overall structural costs, the over-all building costs, and the overall development costs.

t: Pedestrian Evacuation Flow From Hallway to Stairs
l2: T. Chen, W.G. Song, W.C. Fan, S.X. Lu & B. Yao, University of Science and Technology of China
d: Under the conditions of open boundary, pedestrian evacuation dynamics from hallway to stairs is simulated through a lattice-gas model of biased random walkers without back step, whose objective is to pass through the stairs as fast as possible. The effective widths of hallway and stairs in the simulation measure up with the High-rise Building’s Design Fire Codes in China. Given different panic parameters, the density-capacity and density-velocity relations with different gradients of stairs are achieved from the simulation. From the discussion and analysis, it can be concluded that the capacity of a stairs is higher when pedestrian are more motivated to move, and capacity are higher on stairs with a normal gradient that on stairs with a steep gradient. The geometrical structure of stairs affects the critical density, and so as to cause evacuation jams at the junction of the hallway and stairs in high-rise buildings.

t: Preliminary Studies of Fire-Whirl in High-Rise Buildings Using Numerical Simulation
l2: C.K. Chen, B. Yao & W.C. Fan, University of Science and Technology of China
d: After the World Trade Centre disaster, issues on the fire safety of high-rise buildings have become world wide research highlights. Specifically, fire occurring in special structures should be studied. Fire whirl might result under various ventilation conditions and therefore tend to bring higher risk to the occupants and buildings. The paper attempts to study on the occurrence and characteristics of fire-whirl induced by external wind. The theoretical studies were conducted via FDS software. The simulation shows that both velocity of external wind and distribution of ventilators play a dominate role in the occurrence of fire-whirl in high-rise buildings. It as also revealed that under specific conditions, the movement of fire-whirl can be determined and that at high temperature the centre will shift.

t: Economics of Tall Buildings After the World Trade Center Disaster
l2: William R. Code, University of Western Ontario
d: This paper focuses on how the World Trade Center collapse will undoubtedly have significant effects on the development costs of tall buildings, but the greatest consequence of this disaster will likely be its impact on revenue. Changes in rent levels and occupancy rates, more than changes in creation cost, affect the long term financial viability of high rise office developments. It has long been the case that the elevated land and creation costs of central city tall buildings demand higher rental levels. These higher rental levels have traditionally only been supported in the long term by a focused demand for office space, rooted in a real or imagined need to easy face-to-face communications and a willingness to pay some premium for the views and icon status of a tall building.

t: Structural Investigation of the New York World Trade Center Collapse
l2: Gene Corley, CTL Group
d: This paper presents some findings of the FEMA- and SEA/AISA – sponsored investigation into the structural performance of New York’s Trade center following the attacks of September 11, 2001. The structures’ collapse was caused not by aircraft impact alone, but by the combination of impact and the resulting fire that weakened structural members and connections. The preliminary report calls for further study in a number of areas.

t: Advantages of Steel-Concrete-Steel Sandwich Construction in Highrise Buildings
l2: R. Dixon & H.G. Bowerman, Corus Group
d: This paper describes the suitability of Steel-Constructed-Steel sandwich construction for cores in highrise buildings. Its proven performance under single event extreme loads and inherent resistance to a series of such events are discussed. If a structure designed for normal loading is subject to an extreme event, the use of Steel-Concrete-Steel sandwich construction would result in better inherent performance than could be expected from other forms of construction.

t: Innovation of Low Frequency Buildings by Semi-Active Control Using an 'MR' Damper
l2: H. Fujitani, Y. Shiozaki & T. Hiwatashi, Building Research Institute; S. Soda, Waseda University
d: The magneto-rheological (MR) damper is known to control the response of low frequency buildings because of its relatively large force capacity and variable force characteristics. This MR damper generates a claming force which does not depends on the piston speed. The use of this damper improves the safety and functionality of buildings by reducing both response displacements and accelerations. This paper describes the development of a MR damper and the respective control algorithm. The results were validated against shaking table tests on base-isolated test frame.

t: Analyses of Structural Responses and Damages of World Trade Center Tower 2 Due to Impact of Aircraft Crashed
l2: R. Fakuda, E. Fukuzawa, Norihide Koshika, H. Morikawa & et al, Kajima Corporation
d: The September 11, 2001, attacks on WTC1 and 2 caused extensive structural and local damages at the points directly hit by aircrafts. Despite massive local damages to floors,   both towers remained standing for more than about one hour. Although the damages of beams and columns in the exterior perimeter of the towers were reported and published in the ASCE/FEMA report, the actual extent of the damage of the floor system and inner core columns were not actually known. A theoretical investigation was carried out to examine the structural response of the towers, which could help to provide some explanations on why the towers remained standing that long upon direct impact of the aircrafts. Some analyses were undertaken using a simplified model to estimate the overall damage caused while finite element analyses were used to examine local damages and stress conditions.

t: Response of Multi-Story Building Systems Subjected to Blasts and Projected Charges
l2: Ramanatha Ganesh & Enno Koehn, Lamar University
d: Structural design of medium and high rise buildings requires design procedures incorporating blast resistant design of the building in addition to the conventional Dead load & live load and Wind Load & Seismic load design. The principal objective of a blast and impact resistant design is to integrate design procedures that would prevent or retard sudden or delayed failure of the structural element(s) of the building system which would lead to the progressive collapse of the building system and thereby ensuring the availability of sufficient time for evacuation procedures to save lives. This paper exemplifies the modes of paneling of slab systems in buildings and the measures that can incorporated to hold up or prevent them from failing altogether.

t: Measuring Smoke and Toxic Gas Pathways in Tall Buildings
l2: C. Genge, Retrotec Energy Innovations
d: Smoke moves through tall buildings via the unintentional holes that are found in all fire rated walls. Designers assume these holes are small but they are not. Fifteen years of quantitative testing in gaseous extinguishing system enclosures per NFPA2001 has shown that before they are tested, these holes are extremely leaky. Visual location and sealing of holes does not work. The purpose of this paper is to show that these holes are easily measured, located and repaired.

t: Assessment of the Life Safety Aspects and Structural Robustness of High Rise Buildings in Hong Kong
l2: Craig Gibbons & Mingchun Luo, ARUP; David Dumigan, Central Waterfront Property Project Management
d: At the time of the events in New York of September 11, 2001, a 420m high 88 storey office building in Hong Kong was constructed up to the 20th floor level. This paper describes some of the investigations which were undertaken immediately following the attacks to assess overall robustness of the building and other life safety related issues. This paper presents the findings of studies that were undertaken on the resistance of key elements under the effects of impact loading the intense fire scenarios. An important finding from the studies was the importance of dynamic robustness considerations and the beneficial effects of inherent structural ductility and energy absorption in arresting disproportionate collapse.

t: Evacuation Simulation of Tall Buildings
l2: Henri Hakonen, Helsinki University of Technology; Tuomas Susi & Marja-Liisa Siikonen, KONE
d: In this paper, a theoretical review of elevator planning and evacuation by elevators and stairs is made. Elevator and stair simulations were made using the Building Traffic Simulator (BTS). A model used to simulate passenger movement in stairs is presented. Passenger behavior in selecting the fastest way out of the building, as well as considerations of elderly and handicapped people and other passenger groups are explained. The article shows results of evacuation simulations in some high-rise building complexes. The effect of increasing the number of stairs or using elevators in evacuation is analyzed.

t: A Quanitative Method for Calibrating CFD Model Calculations
l2: J.A. Ierardi & J.R. Barnett, Center For Firesafety Studies
d: This paper focuses on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) modeling and how it is being used more frequently in fire protection engineering design. The increase of CFD usage in fire protection engineering can be attributed in part to advances in computer hardware as well as the use of computationally efficient approaches for modeling fire phenomena such as the large eddy simulation technique utilized in NIST’s Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS). The methods presented in this word can be used by CFD modelers to quantify the results of calibration studies as well as to provide guidance on selecting an appropriate grid size when temperature and velocity are the variables of interest.

t: Structural Design and Analysis of Galleria Place
l2: Jong Soo Kim, Song Mun Yoo & Dong Whan Kim, C S Structural Engineering
d: This paper presents the structural design and analysis of the Galleria Palace. As a residential development, this building is located at Jamsil district, Seoul, Korea. It is comprised of 3 towers with 46 stories (149.5 m-high). The towers are currently under construction. The structural system is a dual system and composed of main core wall, outrigger girder, fin wall, and perimeter frame. The perimeter wall was designed for seismic resistance however for resisting lateral loads. The paper  described case study on various combined structural elements in deciding the most effective design for increasing the stiffness of the structures. A detailed analysis of outrigger is also presented.

t: An Assessment of Australian Preparedness for Tall Building Usage in a New Risk Regime
l2: Alan P. Jeary, University of Western Sydney
d: This paper is an assessment of the current state of readiness of Australia to combat terrorist attacks on large occupancy buildings has been assessed in terms of the criteria considered to be key safety issues by the international working group established by the Institution of Structural Engineers in the light of the attacks of September 11th, 2001 in the USA. Nineteen structures in the Eastern states of Australia were assessed in terms of these vital criteria as an indication of the preparedness of the nation to deal with any terrorist assault in that country. The survey was carried out before the October bombings in Bali, and has shown that Australia was generally poor prepared to deal with terrorism. The events in Bali have changed Australian perfections, and some evidence is reported to show that the level of preparedness in Australia has increased significantly since that time.

t: Structural Performance of I-Tech Composite Beam Steel with Web Openings
l2: Young-Kyu Ju, Sung-Chul Chun & Dae-Young Kim, Daewoo Institute of Construction Technology; Do-Hyun Kim & Sae-Dae Kim, Korea University, Kwang-Ryang Chung, Dongyang Structural Engineers
d: In this paper, a newly developed composite beam is proposed and experimentally explored via simple beam tests. The proposed composite beam is named iTECH (Innovative, Technical, Economical, and Convenient Hybrid) beam. iTECH has an asymmetric steel beam assembly with web openings, where the top plate is welded on the top of inverted structural tees cut as “honeycomb” style. The steel assembly is fabricated in the factory and both sides of the web and the slab are filled with cast-in-site concrete. To apply iTECH in practice, its shear and flexural capacities will first have to be evaluated through experiment. From the results of this research, iTECH beam is expected to be properly used as the shallow beam system for high-rise residential buildings.

t: Fire Engineering of Tall Buildings Lessons Learned from 9/11
l2: Martin Kealy, WSP Group
d: This paper addresses two main areas of design; the passive protection of structure and the active protection systems used for escape and fire fighter access. The paper addresses the vulnerability of high rise structures and suggests mitigating measures based on the events of September 11 and the World Trade Centre collapse. The passive protection uses addressed include; real structures in real fires; fire severity and hydrocarbon fires; full burn out calculations and robustness of applied fire protection in high rise environments. Discussions on the design of active systems presented herein include sprinklers; protection of fire fighting shafts; alternatives to stair pressurization and ventilation based on recent UK Building Research Establishment tests and the safe use of lifts for evacuation. The paper concludes that following 9/11 the best way to approach high rise design is by using intelligent fire engineering design from first principles and that is may not be necessary to add more fire protection to a building that has inherent design capabilities.

t: Inflatable Ejection Module (IEM) An Emergency Egress System for Tall Buildings
l2: Anshuman Khanna, University of Southern California
d: The general feeling of skyscrapers being vulnerable and soft targets seems to be repelling people away from this superior architectural typology. One way of administering faith in skyscrapers is by devising an efficient egress system. This paper intends to propose an egress system that would attempt to facilitate rapid severance of the user from the tower at the time of the disaster.

t: Characteristics of Aerodynamic Response of High-Rise Buildings with Open Passage
l2: Hitomitsu Kiktsu & Hisashi Okada, Building Research Institute
d: This paper describes the effectiveness of introducing aerodynamic response control method for mitigating the response and the experimental results of across-wind aerodynamic responses by using several dynamic models with open passage configurations. On the whole, air flow going through open passage can work in effective way to reduce aerodynamic reponses. In this study, the dependence of the aerodynamic responses on the sectional configuration or vertical position of open passage is discussed. From a point of the damping coefficient, sections with along-wind open passage tend to obtain relatively more aerodynamic effect, while across-wind open passages have an adverse influence on the separated sheer layer. As for the vertical height of open passages, aerodynamic responses are the most reduced in the case of open passage introduced on 0.8 to 0.9 of reference height.

t: Comparison of Peak Pressure Coefficients for Wind Load Among International Codes and Standards
l2: Hitomitsu Kiktsu, Hisashi Okada & Yasuo Okuda, Building Research Institute
d: This paper describes the outline of the comparative study on peak pressure coefficients among several international codes and standards. In this study, the adjusted peak pressure coefficients are introduced, which are defined to have unified air density, return period and averaging time of basic wind velocity, reference height and vertical profile. The result of comparisons of external claddings in a high-rise building is presented and the degree of discrepancy among codes and standards are discussed.

t: New Research in the Understanding of Steel Frames in Fire and the Development of New Fire Engineered Solutions Applicable to Tall Buildings
l2: Brian Kirby, Swinden Technology Centre
d: A major fire research programme has been carried out on an 8-storey composite steel frame involving natural fires. The test arrangements and results are described and the important factors that control the structural deformation processes are discussed. One of the main conclusions from this work is that modern composite steel frames are extremely robust in fire in the event of loss in strength of individual steel members. Some of the numerical modeling carried out is described and the resulting design guidance that is now emerging applicable to tall buildings is presented. Comments are also made with regard to the behaviour of bolts and welds at connections.

t: Towards a Better and Safer Built Environment
l2: Ron Klemencic, CTBUH
d: This paper discusses the need to educate the ordinary consumer about their built environments and the buildings in which they live and work. Most people know very little about the structures in which they spend the majority of their time. However, in the aftermath 9/11, there has been widespread questioning of the safety and structural integrity of our buildings. CTBUH has taken a leadership role in representing all disciplines concerned with the built environment and educating the general public so that appropriate expectations can be formulated concerning building performance in response to various hazards and threats.

t: An Overview of Elevator Use for Emergency Evacuation
l2: JOhn H. Klote, John H. Klote, Inc. - Fire and Smoke Consulting
d: Throughout most of the world, warning signs next to elevators indicate that they should not be used in fire situations. However, the idea of using elevators for fire evacuation has gained considerable attention. This paper is an overview of what has been learned from a number of research projects conducted at the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) in the 1908s and 1990s concerning the use of elevators during building fires.

t: Role of Fire Resistance Issues in the Collapse of the Twin Towers
l2: Venkatesh Kodur, National Research Council
d: This paper covers the twin towers of the World Trade Centre and how it suffered significant damage from the impact of the planes, however they withstood the impact. The severe fires that followed the impact brought down the twin towers, WTC 2 and WTC 1, at different intervals – 59 and 89 minutes respectively following the impact. Thus, fire issues played a major part in the collapse of the twin towers and the role of various fire resistance issues is examined in this paper.

t: Fire-Resistance of Large Steel Columns Under Axial Load
l2: Mamoru Kohno & Hideaki Masuda, Building Research Institute
d: Some experimental investigations into the fire resistance properties of axially loaded large steel columns are described herein. The columns were subjected to fire with a temperature profile as specified in ISO 834 and hydrocarbon standard fire curve, both of which are conventional testing and design practices. Tests were conducted to examine the performance of large steel columns with and without fire protection. It is concluded that loses for a 3-hour rated fire protection the column could withstand about 10 hours before failing. However, loses in fire protection would significantly reduce the fire resistance.

t: Catastrophic Fire Supression and Safe Evacuation in High-Rise Buildings Using Breathable Fire - Suppressive Atmosphere
l2: Igor Kotliar, FirePASS Corp.
d: This paper describes fire prevention and suppression system which can suppress large and catastrophic fire then enable occupants in high-rise buildings to evacuate safely. This system can be engineered for preventive or suppressive mode or combination of both and applicable to new buildings and for retrofitting of existing ones. The technology allows construction of high and large buildings with the highest level of fire safety.

t: Elevators for Occupant Evacuation and Fire Department Acces
l2: Erica Kuligowski, National Institute of Standards and Technology
d: This paper will present a study of the potential for elevators to reduce occupant egress time and fire department access time for fires in tall buildings. Potential reductions in egress and access time will be compared for some specific cases where times for current procedures are available. The paper will review past research in elevator usage and the structure of existing models for elevator evacuation. From this review, the assumptions and limitations of the current elevator and egress models will be discussed, and future plans for improvement of the existing egress prediction techniques will be presented.

t: The Development of an Evaluation Systemn for Structural Damage
l2: Kouichi Kusunoki & Masaomi Teshigawara, Building Research Institute
d: In order to reduce additional damage due to an aftershock, a quick inspection on the damaged buildings must be carried out. However, the buildings have to be entirely investigated by engineers or researchers under existing situation. The judgment made can vary accordingly to the engineers’ experiences and usually takes a long time to investigate all damaged buildings. Furthermore, it is very difficult to conduct visual inspection on tall buildings. This research aims to develop a new automatic and quick inspection system that requires only several accelerometers. This system makes it possible to obtain the information of safety level against an aftershock to professional immediately.

t: Fire Resistance - Implications for Regulations and Standings of the September 11th Terrorist Attacks on the World Trade Center
l2: Tom Lennon, Fire Risk Services
d: This paper will consider the concept of fire performance and fire resistance in terms of traditional prescriptive fire design and in relation to performance based fire engineering design. Results from a series of full scale fire tests carried out on real buildings at BRE’s whole building test facility will be used to look at the effectiveness of fire engineering design methods.

t: Whole Buildings Behaviour: Results From a Series of Large Scale Tests
l2: Tom Lennon, Fire Risk Services
d: Structural integrity and stability during and immediately following a fire are traditionally maintained through a reliance on the performance of individual members (beams, columns, walls) subject to idealised loading conditions during a standard fire test. The application of performance based codes for fire engineering rely on the availability of accurate source data related to material properties at elevated temperature and aspects of loading and structural performance. The work carried out at BRE goes some way to provide this information for real buildings subject to large natural fires.

t: Procedures for Progressive Collapse Analysis
l2: Shalva Marjanishvili, Hinman Consulting Engineers
d: Following the collapse of the World Trade Center towers in September 2001, there has been heightened interest among building owners and government entities in evaluating the progressive collapse potential of existing buildings and in designing new buildings to resist this type of collapse. This paper presents four successive and increasingly more sophisticated analysis procedures for evaluating progressive collapse hazard namely linear-elastic static, non-linear static, linear-elastic dynamic and non-linear dynamic.

t: A Risk-Informed Performance-Based Approach for Tall Building Design
l2: Brian Meacham, Ove Arup & Partners
d: In the weeks and months following the collapse of the World Trade Center Towers 1 and 2 as a result of a terrorist attack, there has been significant discussion relative to whether and/or how the design of very tall building may need to change to become more resilient. In many respects, this line of discussion can be easily understood, give the tragic loss of life and destruction of a global icon. This paper outlines a risk-informed performance-based analysis and design process and illustrates how it can be applied to meet the varied objectives of multiple stakeholders.

t: Equal Opportunity in Evacuations in Future Tall Buildings
l2: John Ng, Escape Consult Mobiltex
d: The future tall buildings belong to building owners who see the need to adopt new solutions to provide access as well as equal egress for building occupants and their visitors. Such solutions would give people with physical disabilities the same ability that able-bodied people have for vertical exits during emergency evacuation. The lack of emergency egress provision for this segment of society in the event of high-rise building evacuation continues to be an ethical issue.

t: Life Safety System Design for the Petronas Twin Towers
l2: Lawrence S. Ng, Cesar Pelli & Associates
d: One of the early design meetings of the Petronas Twin Towers was held in New York on the date of the first explosion at the World Trade Center in February 1993, when a truck packed with explosives detonated in the basement of the North Tower. The World Trade Towers suffered limited structural damage from the explosion. Most of the chaos was caused by the confusion in the evacuation. In view of this face, the consultant team of the Petronas Twin Towers therefore focused its attention on the enhancement of life safety systems, to ensure an expedient and safe evacuation process in the event of emergency.

t: Vulnerability Assessment of Concrete Tall Buildings Subjected to Extreme Loading Conditions
l2: Tuan Ngo, Priyan Mendis & Gideon Hadi Kusuma, University of Melbourne
d: This paper presents a vulnerability/survivability assessment procedure based on the analysis of a typical tall building in Australia. The structural stability and integrity of the building was assessed by considering the effects of the failure of some perimeter columns, spandrel beams and floor slabs due to blast overpressure or impact. The authors suggest some methods to improve the impact resistance of concrete walls and slabs, as well as the rotation capacity of the beams, columns and joints which have been found from this study of crucial importance for collapse prevention.

t: The Prediction Method for an External Flame Ejected From an Opening of a Building
l2: Yoshifumi Ohmiya, Yoshihiko Hayashi & Shuitsu Yusa, Building Research Institute; Michael A. Delichatsios, University of Ulster
d: This paper covers the work that is part of a larger effort to quantify the fire hazard from fully involved enclosure fires having an opening. It specifically addresses the burning behavior of pyrolysing gases, their flame extent and the heat fluxes from flames emerging outside the enclosure. An experimental study is described using a small-scale enclosure having an prescribed fire source to investigate at steady state conditions the behavior of flames emerging from an opening enclosure.

t: Studies on the Design Earthquake Motions in Accordance to the Revised Japanese Buildings Standards
l2: Izuru Okawa, Mitsumasa Midorikawa, Masanori Iiba & Toshihide Kashima, Building Research Institute; Shin Koyama, National Institute for Land and Infrastructure Management
d: The Japanese Building Standard has been revised to take into considerations of new concepts in defining the seismic design forces. This paper describes the concepts of defining the design ground motions in terms of the response spectrum, time history and hazard levels corresponding to structural performance.

t: Design of Structures for Fire in the Australian Regulatory Environment and the Impact of the WTC Collapse
l2: David Proe, Peter Johnson & Richard Hough, ARUP
d: This paper summarizes the regulations and design methods applicable to the fire design of high-rise structures in Australia and discusses the effect of the WTC collapse on the building design and development community in Australia.

t: Evacuation of the World Trade Center: What Went Right?
l2: Guylène Proulx, National Research Council Canada; Rita F. Fahy, National Fire Protection Association
d: The evacuation of the World Trade Center on September 11, 2001 was not the first evacuation of the 100-storey high towers. Partial and full evacuations had taken place on other occasions. One particular event was the full evacuation of the two towers on Friday February 26, 1993 when a bomb exploded in the underground garage. The comparison of the 1993 and 2001 evacuation are compared to the analysis of the first-person accounts found in the media following the 2001 disaster. It is concluded that the two full evacuations differ substantially.

t: Performance Strategies for Highrise Buildings: Tendecies and Developments in Europe
l2: Nobert Rehle & Werner Sobek, Werner Sobek Ingenieure
d: This paper focuses on consequences of a presumed collapse for the design of high-rise buildings in Europe. Some recently developed high-rise buildings in Germany will be presented to exemplify that there needs to be no contradiction between a safe and a transparent building.

t: Structural Design of Tall Buildings with a Minimal Risk of Collapse
l2: Erik Reitzel, Technical University of Denmark
d: This paper is concerned with a new strategy for designing tall buildings: The implementation of flexibility in strategically chosen zones of the construction instead of strength. By integrating zones of shock absorbers into the bearing structures of tall buildings the risk of collapse can be minimized. If there zones of shock absorbers are combined with minimal structures it will be possible to construct tall building of more than 100 floors secured against terror. The most efficient combination is obtained by using strategy of hierarchic order for the structural system.

t: Facade Emergency Exits Concept
l2: Jose Romano, Jose Romano Arquitectos
d: This paper highlights one of the most important safety measures in every design is the provision of means of evacuation in ensuring people to escape quickly to a safe area in a short time from any point of the building.

t: Interaction of Height-Dependent Performances Properties for Facades of Tall Buildings
l2: Jonathan Sakua, Halcrow Yolles; Frederick Hulton, QinetiQ
d: The design and specification of cladding and glazing for tall buildings differs from that in lower buildings, in that is needs to take into account many height-related performance characteristics. These include wind, sound and impact requirements, and also issues such as clearances to allow for racking and other effects. The growing trend towards the design of significant buildings for resistance against blast loading introduces a further variable. This paper explores how these different requirements vary, depending on the height above ground.

t: Fire Resistance of Steel Frames
l2: Yoshifumi Sakumoto, Nippon Steel; Taro Nishigaki, Taisei; Koji Ikeda, Shimizu
d: This paper presents an analysis on the fire resistance of the World Trade Center’s steel frames and examines precautions to be taken in designing skyscrapers.

t: Evacuation Design Strategy for High-Rise Buildings
l2: Tomoharu Sano & Ichiro Hagiwara, National Institute for Land and Infrastructure Management; Atsuko Tanaka, Agency of Industrial Science and Technology
d: This paper studies the data on human behavior that was collected and analyzed from reports of mass media and interviews from survivors of the World Trade Center which collapsed on September 11, 2001. The escape behavior shows some problems to be reconsidered on the evacuation design strategy for high-rise buildings. For solving these problems on evacuation from high-rise buildings, a new design strategy is required for safety evacuation.

t: An Analysis of the Constraints to Innovation in High-Rise Buildings
l2: Frits Scheublin, Hollandsche Beton Groep
d: The Dutch Association of contractors (VGBouw) initiated a study to investigate the constraints to innovation in high rise building in the Netherlands. The study is intended to investigate why high rise building, despite its great repetition of activities and its huge investment is not as industrialized as on may expect. The development and construction of 3 recent projects were investigated.

t: Recent Supertall Buildings in Korea and Reinforced Concrete Structural System for a 130 Story Ultra High-Rise Building
l2: Sung-Woo Shin, Beom-Seok Han & Min-Sung Song, Hanyang University Seoul
d: The studies described recent developments of tall building construction in Korea and some research findings on the design of reinforced concrete structures based on the usage of high strength concrete. This paper describes the variables related to the design to tall buildings namely its structural systems location of outriggers, size of members and concrete strength. The performance is evaluated in terms of inter-storey drifts, maximum lateral displacements economics considerations and usage of materials.

t: Unitary Load-Equalization in the Solution of Linearly Elastic Structures and Unified Damage Analysis
l2: Satya P. Singh, Transportation Technology Center
d: This paper presents the development of a new methodology for the solution of simultaneous linear equations. The method bypasses the need of inverting the coefficient matrix of its usual rove transformation of the use of iterative schemes. The convergence of solution is guaranteed when the diagonal of the coefficient matrix is dominant. Singularity in the coefficient matrix is treated to mean the damage can be made by using this method.

t: Enhancing the Safety of High Risk Buildings Through the Concept of Levels of Safety and Use of Fire Engineering Tool
l2: Raphaël Slama, Conseil général des Ponts et Chaussées
d: The document presents the reflections of a working group of limited duration, convened by the author after the attacks on the World Trade Center (WTC). The resulting fire has shown the limits of the current concept of safety for addressing premises presenting a very high density of risks. Is has stressed the need to use the more specific and multidimensional concept of “level of safety”, corresponding to various risk factors and assessed through fire engineering tools.

t: Performance: Imperative for Safety, Security, and Competitiveness in the 21st Century
l2: Jack Snell, National Institute of Standards and Technolgoy
d: Following the collapse of the World Trade Center (WTC) towers on September 11, 2001, NIST developed a Public-Private Response Plan. It consists of an investigation of the collapses, an R&D program to transform the findings into actionable recommendations, and a dissemination program to engage industry and government into implementation of recommendations. The elements of the plan are now well underway.

t: Fire Protection Engineering: Where are We Heading from Here? A Look at Present and Future Research Agendas in Tall Buildings
l2: Robert Solomon, National Fire Protection Association
d: The safety of occupants in the tall building environment has been a primary concern since multiple story buildings were advocated. As new structures “towered” to 10 stories (100ft; 31m) in the late 1800’s, a natural concern was for the management of a fire event. How can the occupants be protected? How can the structure be protected? What systems could be provided to automatically intervene during a fire event? How can manual fire suppression operations be conducted in these buildings?

t: Hostile Acts as a Design Approach
l2: Robert Solomon, National Fire Protection Association
d: The next decade will introduce a new generation of code specified criteria in building codes and life safety codes in the United States as well as other countries. This criteria will be intended to address a new realm of hazards that have previously not been applied to most buildings and structures. Although highly specialized building design criteria has existed for many years, its application and practice has largely been reserved for use on select structures built and occupied by the US Department of Defense and the US Department of State.

t: Numerical Analysis of Wind Loads on Tall Buildings in Urban Area
l2: Tetsuro Tamura, Susumu Kono, Shuyang Cao & Makoto Tsubokura, Tokyo Institute of Technology; Hisashi Okada & Yasuo Okuda, Building Research Institute
d: Wind affects tall buildings in various ways. The wind speed increase with altitudes therefore wind loading on tall buildings increases with height. The wind load estimation requires the information obtained from time-dependent unsteady flow, such as turbulence, intensity, spectra and instantaneous maximum value for wind velocity, etc. From the numerical technique point of view, the large eddy simulation (LES) approach is most suitable, and therefore used in the studies described here.

t: Revision of the AIJ Recommendations on Wind Loads for Buildings
l2: Yukio Tamura, Tokyo Polytechnic University; Takeshi Ohkuma, Kanagawa University; Hikaru Kawai, Kyoto University
d: This paper presents the revised draft version of the wind load provisions of the AIJ Recommendations for buildings (AIJRLB). One of the major revisions is the introduction of wind direction factors. If the building orientation is properly engineered, taking into account of wind directions, then an economical design may become possible. However, in this case, the combination of the along-wind force, across-wind force and torsional forces should be taken into account in the structural design not only for high-rise buildings but also for low- and medium-rise buildings.

t: The Structural Redundancy of Earthquake Resistant Designed Buildings Against Impulsive Load
l2: Kazuo Tamura, Yutaka Nakamura & Daigo Ishii, Shimizu Corporation
d: Response analyses of tall buildings subjected to airplane impact were performed and the effects were compared with the seismic and wind design loads. Building structural redundancy was examined for total collapse of a tall building which loses some structural elements, such as columns. Response analysis results for multi-story shear buildings subjected to simplified impulsive loads indicate that the longer the duration of the input impulsive loads, the more the response acceleration decreases, but the story drifts, story shear forced and overturning moments increase. The possibility of total building collapse due to airplane impact, which initially induces the collapse of some floors, is found to be related to overall building redundancy.

t: Development of Structural Safety Monitoring System for Buildings
l2: Masaomi Teshigawara & Hiroshi Isoda, Building Research Institute; K. Kitagawa, Keio University
d: The objective of this paper is to propose a hybrid structural safety monitoring device incorporating a sensing and analytical modeling system. The structural safety monitoring device for an 8-story, Steel Reinforced Concrete (SRC) building is proposed and discussed. The system composed of four parts; Sensing, Analytical, Modeling and Display.

t: Protection Against Biological and Chemical Agents
l2: Jari Vepsalainen, Lifa Air Asia
d: A building is a system of barriers which protect occupants from the environment. However the building system is incomplete in that it contains openings upon which winds, fan and natural convection act to exchange indoor air with outdoor air. These opening are both intention such as windows, doors, vents and outside air intakes. Unintentional openings include cracks, joints, seams and pores.

t: Effective and Efficient Building Control
l2: I. Visscher & F.M. Meijer, Delft University of Technology; L. Sheridan, University of Liverpool
d: Building regulations provide a solid base for safe, healthy, energy efficient, environment friendly and comfortable buildings. To be sure that the requirements are met in design and in construction, an appropriate system of building control is indispensable. In the Netherlands some recent striking accidents with buildings have put the importance of the quality is beyond acceptable. Besides the effectiveness of the control system, also the efficiency of permit procedures and actions of plan checking and site inspection has been an important drive to search for alternative policies.

t: A Study on the Redundancy of High-Rise Steel Buildings
l2: Akira Wada, Tokyo University; Kenichi Ohi, Kobe University; Hiroaki Suzuki, University of Tsukuba; Yoshitsugu Sakamoto & Mitsuo Fushimi, Nippon Steel; Hisaya Kamura, JFE Steel
d: This paper presents a theoretical investigation of the redundancy capacity of steel frames due to heat induced by fire and loss of columns due to impacts from explosions and plane crash. A non-linear analysis was used to estimate the redundancy capacity through examination of the axial load utilization ratio of columns.

t: Fire Resistance Design Criteria of Tall Building Structures
l2: Yong C Wang, University of Manchester
d: This paper argues that the structural fire resistant design criteria of tall building structures should be considered differently from those embedded in the current philosophy where only stability of the structure in fire is considered. The potential reparability issue should be addressed at the design stage by conducting a potential risk assessment. This risk assessment may determine the acceptable extent of fire damage to a tall building that the fire resistant design team should aim to achieve.

t: Numerical Study on Possiblity of Activating Smoke Detectors in an Atrium
l2: H.B. Wang, B. Yao, L. Yi, R. Huo, C.L. Shi & Y.Z. Li, University of Science and Technology of China
d:
Atriums can be found in many high-rise buildings and detecting an atrium fire is very difficult because of the large space, e.g. the thermal-sensing head cannot be easily activated by the low smoke temperature due to the large air entrainment. The commonly acceptable method is to install an aspirated smoke detection system at the atrium ceiling to detect an early stage fire. 
Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS) developed at the NIST was used in the present paper to study the possibility of activating a smoke detector in an atrium with hot air layer by numerically simulating the air movement induced by an early stage fire.

t: Performance Design Review and Verification Techniques
l2: Robert Weber, R. D. Weber & Associates
d: This paper is presented to discuss the building official’s role in reviewing performance-based designs and methods used to verify compliance with adopted codes. Architects and engineers may have an option in a jurisdiction of submitting a performance-based design in accordance with the ICC Performance Code for when authorized by the building official.

t: Mitigation of Backdraft with Water Mist: A Reduced-Scale Experimental Study
l2: W.G. Weng, W.C. Fan & B, Yao, University of Science and Technology of China
d: Fire is always a hazard that threatens the safety of tall buildings. A recent incident is the collapse of the World Trade Center. Backdraft is a special condition in a limited-ventilation fire. It can develop from fires of either ordinary combustibles or flammable liquids that become oxygen starved yet continue to generate a fuel-rich environment. If abundant fresh air is abruptly supplied by opening a door or breaking a window, the hot gas in this vitiated building will rapidly combust and a fire ball and a blast wave will take place.

t: Behaviour of Glass Fibre Reinforced Plastics (GRP) Columns at Elevated Temperatures
l2: PMH Wong, Yong Wang & J Michael Davies, University of Manchester
d: This paper presents the results of a series of tests studying the compression behavior of pultruded 100 x 50 x 4 mm Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP) C-shaped channels at different elevated temperatures. A total of 42 tests were conducted, i.e. 27 tests about the minor axis of the section and 15 tests about the major axis.

t: Integrated Structural Fire Design for High Rise Buildings
l2: Young Wong & Neal Butterworth, Buro Happold FEDRA
d: This paper presents a fire safety design approach integrating evacuation, structural performance, fire fighting and fire safety management. An integrated performance based design with time as a basis of measurement is discussed. Such an approach allows increased design flexibility and enables engineers to predict the level of safety in each design and thereby derive the most cost effective and safe solution. A project example is also presented along with further improvements for the future. A high level of computer modeling was utilized during the design, which requires additional care and understanding by the designers and enforcers to ensure their validity.

t: To Live High or Low: Case Study of Singapore Public Housing Residents
l2: Belinda Yuen, Steve Appold & Kwee Lanny Kurnianingrum, The National University of Singapore; Anthony Yeh, University of Hong Kong; George Earl, University of Queensland; John Ting, Private Architect
d: The intent of this paper is to discuss the empirically derived research on Singapore residents’ perceptions of building height and their reactions to supertall living. More specifically, it will seek to address: Do residents prefer high or low floor living? Why? What do they seek in tall building living? What concerns them most in tall building living? At what height do they think an apartment building began to be tall?

t: Analysis of Simultaneous Evacuation of the World Trade Center
l2: Yoshikatsu Yoshida, Takenaka Corporation
d: The studies described herein examined the evacuation during the World Trade Center in New York in an attempt to seek measure for life safety design in future tall buildings. A notable point during the escape from the Towers was heavy congestion at the stairs. This paper presents a dynamic simulation of evacuation using Fluid Model to study the evacuation of the two Towers. The human and physical parameters for these studies were obtained from the internet and the results of analyses compared to the conventional static method.

t: A Study on the Redundancy of High-Rise Steel Buildings Due to the Effect of Induced Heat and Loss of Vertical Structural Members
l2: A. Wada, Tokyo Insitute of Technology; K. Ohi, University of Tokyo; H. Suzuki, Tsukuba University; et al.
d: This paper presents a theoretical investigation of the redundancy capacity of steel frames due to heat induced by fire and loss of column due to impacts from explosions and plane crashes.

t: "Pavements in the Sky": Use of the Skybridge in Tall Buildings
l2: Antony Wood, University of Nottingham
d: Improved safety in tall buildings has become a major issue. The high level ‘skybridge’ – an old idea – is one solution which could be applied to both new and existing buildings and enrich our cities.

t: Building Regulations on Fire Safety in Europe
l2: L. Sheridan, University of Liverpool, England; IR. H.J. Visscher & F.M. Meijer, Delft University of Technology, The Netherlands
d: This paper focuses on the conclusions of the analysis of the fire safety requirements. Comparisons proved particularly difficult due to the difference in the structure, description and content of the requirements, which may reflect the long history of the regulation of fire safety.

t: Performance System Model - A Framework For Describing The Totality of Building Performance
l2: B. Meacham, Ove Arup & Partners; B. Tubbs, International Code Council; D. Bergeron, National Research Council; F. Szigeti, International Centre for Facilities
d: This paper outlines a performance system model for describing the integration of components in a performance-based building regulatory system and for helping to establish a common stakeholder understanding of total building performance.

t: High-rise with Cable Net Facade for the 21st Century!
l2: Abbas Aminmansour, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, USA
d: This article offers a synopsis of some of the unique features of the building and offers an insight into the collaboration between team members involved in the design and construction of the building.

t: Effective Stiffness Modeling of Shear Walls for Seismic Analysis of High-rise Structures
l2: Mark Sarkisian, Neville Mathias, Cem Ozer & Jean-Pierre Chakar, SOM
d: The design of the lateral load resisting systems in highrise structures is very often governed by the requirements of drift control. This is true even in buildings with relatively rigid lateral systems that include shear walls. Appropriate modeling of the effective stiffness properties of shear walls thus plays an important role in determining the sizes of these shear walls when used, with the accompanying impacts on construction costs, available rentable space, and nonstructural components.

t: Optimal Control of Double-Decker Elevator Group Using Genetic Algorithm
l2: Janne Sorsa & Marja-Liisa Siikonen, KONE
d: We shall introduce the principles of optimal routing of double-deck elevators. Elevator routing problem is formulated as an integer programming problem and it is solved using a genetic algorithm in a real time system.

t: Tall Building Fire Safety - Post 9/11
l2: Joe Zicherman, Fire Cause Analysis
d: It is the objective of this paper to address evacuation issues under extreme conditions, which are a component of this problem area.

t: Shoring Wall and Subsurface Structural Systems: IDX Tower, Seattle, Washington
l2: David G. Winter, Shannon & Wilson; Douglas E. Loesch, MKA; Robert Hollister, Hines
d: This paper reports on the performance of the excavation shoring. The new building is supported on a mat footing that varies in thickness from 6 to 14 feet. This paper also discusses the design of the mat, and the building core wall support.

t: Protective Design: Saving Lives Through Structural Engineering
l2: Tod Rittenhouse & Robert Smilowitz, Weidlinger Associates Consulting Engineers
d: In recent years the engineering community has had to consider new design criteria, the terrorist explosive threat. Whether developing new or renovation projects, protective design and dynamic analysis has become part of the structural engineering service.

t: What Does September 11th Mean for Building Structure Design
l2: Jon Magnusson, MKA
d: The horrible events of September 11th have aroused many emotions and created many questions in the minds of the public, the media, political leaders, and design professionals. For officials charged with developing building codes and designers charged with creating buildings the question is very specific: Is there something wrong with our codes and can we change them so that this doesn’t happen again?

t: Engineering Systems an Incremental Response to Terrorist Threat
l2: Norman D. Kurtz & Andrew Hlushko, Flack + Kurtz Inc; Dan Nall, Director of Advanced Building Sciences
d: The design approach of a new building, or safety assessment of an existing one, must look at the overall performance of the integrated building systems. The performance of the structural systems, curtain wall, security systems, mechanical, electrical, fire protection systems, etc. must be analyzed against the threat and perceived risks.

t: Where Do We Go From Here? - In the Aftermath of the World Trade Center Attacks
l2: Ron Klemencic, MKA
d: Ron Klemencic writes on the 9/11 attacks and the problem architects, engineers, planners and building officials face as problem solvers.

t: Emergency Evacuation Core Proposal: 25 May 2002
l2: Jeffrey Heller, Heller Manus Architects
d: This proposal for an enhanced emergency core was inspired by the images of the courageous firefighters climbing the stairs of the World Trade Center against the torrent of occupants pouring down the stairs.

t: Educating the Public of Safety in Tall Buildings
l2: Jeffery E. Harper, RJA
d: Since the terrorist attacks on September 11 to the twin towers of the World Trade Center, many questions have been asked, by both building designers and the general public, regarding what could have been done differently during the design or construction of the WTC to have changed the outcome of that day. What could have been done to increase the time for evacuating the buildings’ occupants?

t: Strategies for Increasing Student Numbers in Architectural Design Studio
l1: CIB International Conference on Building Education and Research, April 2003
l2: Antony Wood, Swinal Samant, Paola Sassi & Adam Sharr, University of Nottingham
d: This paper considers the impact of increasing student numbers and decreasing resources on the teaching of design in schools of architecture. The provenance of these changes is considered and merits of studio based learning are discussed.

t: Demystifying Construction; A Case Study of Construction Teaching in the UK
l1: CIB International Conference on Building Education and Research, April 2003
l2: Antony Wood, University of Nottingham
d: Construction is often seen, amongst both students and staff at Schools of Architecture, as a distinct subject taught separately from design. It is often delivered by lectures, divorced from design studio and relying heavily on published sources. Even when it is integrated into design studio, it is often conducted as an add-on project, a separate exercise set after the design is ‘finished’.

t: Seismic Design and Detailing of Compound Shear Wall Plan Configurations
l2: Mark Sarkisian, Neville Mathias & Eric Long, SOM
d: The Uniform Building Code and ACI offer only limited guidance for the design and detailing of shear walls of compound configurations such as “I”, “L”, “C”, and “T” shapes in plan. There is a dearth of examples for seismic design and detailing of such shear walls, particularly for high-rise buildings in zones of high seismicity in available references.

t: Development of fire detection systems in the intelligent building
l2: Z.G. Liu, J.M. Makar & A.K. Kim, National Research Council Canada
d: This paper reviews the current state of the art for fire detection and alarm systems in intelligent buildings. It identifies new technologies and concepts developed for intelligent buildings that could be used to improve the capability of fire safety systems.

t: Fire Induced Progressive Collapse
l1: National Workshop on the Prevention of Progressive Collapse Chicago 2002
l2: David Scott, Barbara Lane & Craig Gibbons, ARUP
d: This paper considers issues related to fire induced progressive collapse of tall buildings in extreme events. It discusses current fire engineering practice, particularly as it relates to tall building design and the issues that need to be considered in the understanding and prevention of progressive collapse.

t: Early Days of North American Wind Engineering: An Interview with Professor Cermak about Professor Davenport
l2: Leighton Cochran, Cermak Peterka Peterson, Inc.
d: Leighton Cochran interviewed Professor Jack Cermak about Professor Davenport's accomplishments.

t: Efficient evacuation methods in tall buildings
l2: Marja-Liisa Siikonen & Henri Hakonen, KONE Corporation
d: The speed with which people can be rescued from a building can be crucial in saving lives in many emergency situations. In this article, evacuation studies have been made for an ideal situation where no smoke effects, or fire, and no damage to the building is considered.

t: New Trends in Elevatoring Solutions for Medium to Medium-High Buildings to Improve Flexibility
l2: Johannes de Jong & Marja-Liisa Siikonen, KONE
d: This article explains in detail alternative elevator arrangements available at the moment. Their consequences on the traffic handling in the building and the space required are considered. This article will also explain the different selection criteria required for each different elevator system.

t: Integrating Architecture and Structural Form in Tall Steel Building Design
l2: Aysin Sev, Mimar Sinan University
d: This paper places the integration of structural and architectural design in an historical perspective and discusses the current approaches to the integration of structural and architectural design for high-rise buildings.

t: Soil-Structure Interaction Effects of Damped Structures
l2: Wenshen Pong, San Francisco State University; C.S. Tsai, Feng-Chia University, Taiwan
d: This paper presents a rigorous time-domain procedure to address the interaction effects of structures equipped with fluid viscous dampers and foundations with an unbounded medium. Quantitative results show that, during earthquakes, there are significant differences between a system with and without radiation damping.

t: Honeycomb Welded Structures: An Ideal Concept of Metal Structures for the 21st Century
l2: Koichiro Okuto, Okuto Studio of Metal Structures,
d: Many new technical innovations in the building industry have occurred in the 20th century. Complexities in buildings that arose out of new demands of the buildings’ function and form led to explorations in technology to develop novel construction techniques, materials, and structural systems. This paper describes one such innovation that could improve the performance of metal structures with a promise for practical application.

t: Urban Quality of Life Evaluation Scenarios: The Case of Sao Carlos in Brazil
l2: Jose F. G. Mendes, University of Minho; Wilson Satoro Motizuki, University of Sao Paulo
d: This paper is about the second level of evaluation, the one that is concerned with urban features. Special emphasis is given to the use of multicriteria evaluation of QoL, including the incorporation of fuzzy set membership functions in criteria standardization, and to procedures for criterion aggregation in the presence of varying degrees of trade-off and risk attitude.

t: Economic Considerations for Tall Multi-Use Buildings
l2: Robert M. Lau, Roosevelt University
d: There are many recent proposals for tall high-rises that are under consideration or have been discarded. Even though high-rises do cost large sums of money, they are generally efficient on a costper- square-foot basis for moderate heights.

t: Studies on Serviceability of Concrete Structures under Static and Dynamic Loads
l2: Ding Dajun & An Lin,  Nanjing Institute of Technology
d: This paper presents a series of experimental and theoretical studies on the serviceability of concrete structures, conducted at Nanjing Institute of Technology (NIT) for more than 35 years. They include studies of cracking and deformations of concrete members under static loading and of deformations under dynamic loading.

t: Interaction Between a Box-Foundation and a Super-Frame
l2: Ding Dajun, Nanjing Institute of Technology
d: This paper deals with the mechanism of interaction between a box-foundation and a superstructure frame, referred to here as a super-frame. The author proposes an alternative to a currently used calculation method (Meyerhof’s method) and puts forward his own preliminary proposals for calculating the reinforcement requirements.

t: Analysis and Model Techniques for Determining Dynamic Behaviour of Civil Structures with Various Damping Systems
l2: B. Breukelman, T. Haskett, S. Gamble, & P. Irwin, RWDI
d: Controlling vibrations of structures through the use of damping systems continues to increase as structures become lighter and more slender. As a result, the tools used for design of these supplemental damping systems need to take into account the nature of the applied loads to get a more accurate determination of the way the structure will behave with the damping system.

t: Recent Applications of Damping Systems for Wind Response
l2: Peter Irwin & Brian Breukelman, RWDI
d: A traditional solution to the problem of excessive motion of tall buildings under wind action is to add more structure so as to stiffen the building and increase the mass. However, another approach is to supplement the damping of the structure. Some of the first buildings where this approach was taken were in North America.

t: Liberating Urban Architecture (The Merging of the Virtual and the Real)
l2: Peter Pran, Peter Pran + H Architects LLC
d: Visionary creativity and innovative thinking in architecture and engineering are liberating us and allowing us to develop outstanding new solutions for buildings and urban design. Our close architect-engineer-developer collaboration and trust makes everything possible.

t: The Emirates Towers
l2: Hazel W. S. Wong, NORR Group
d: Proposed winning competition design by Hazel W. S. Wong  (Fig. 1) realized the objective of His Highness Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, Crown Prince of Dubai through the creation of twin slender towers, carefully located to pay homage to an existing landmark, and flanked at each tower base by low curvilinear buildings, in forms reminiscent of massive, shifting sand dunes, housing parking and service elements.

t: Recent Infrastructure Developments in Hong Kong – the Background, Current and Future Developments
l2: Raymond W. M. Wong, City University of Hong Kong
d: To accommodate somewhat 7 million people in a piece of land of size slightly bigger than 1050 sq km like Hong Kong is not an easy task. This paper aims to provide a summary of what has been done in the recent years as a means of strategic improvement to the territory of Hong Kong.

t: Emerging Trends in Retail and Entertainment Developments
l2: Ro Shroff, Callison
d: As the world gets more urbanized and cities continue to be the experimental grounds for changing lifestyles, it is an appropriate juncture to step back, evaluate and prognosticate as to what these developments mean and portend.

t: The Future of High-Rise Buildings
l2: George Schipporeit, Illinois Institute of Technology
d: For anyone interested in the relationship of urban issues and tall buildings, there is no greater challenge than this 6th World Congress theme of “Cities and the Third Millennium.” The last few decades leading up to this threshold of time have demonstrated an unprecedented acceleration of transformation and change.

t: Transportation in the Built Environment: Cities in the Third Millennia
l2: Robert Prieto, Fluor
d: The survival of cities and perhaps humanity itself is increasingly linked to our ability to move people and goods in an environmentally, socially and economically sustainable manner.

t: Sustainability and Cities: The Role of Tall Buildings in this New Global Agenda
l2: Peter Newman, Curtin University
d: Sustainability in cities is defined in terms of the reducing of resource inputs and waste outputs whilst simultaneously improving livability. This definition is traced through the recent history of global politics and is then applied to two case studies: Australian settlements and a squatter settlement in Jakarta.

t: Tall Buildings in Melbourne: Challenging Policy Frameworks
l2: Lyndsay Neilson, Nielsen Associates
d: This paper provides a brief overview of the history of tall buildings in Melbourne, Australia, and discusses the policy context within which they have developed through various phases.

t: The Role of Accessibility for the Success of City Centres
l2: Rolf Monheim, University of Bayreuth
d: World-wide discussions about the success of city centres are focused on their accessibility. Generally, it is understood first of all as the accessibility for driving and parking cars.

t: Geologic and Seismic Hazards in the Urban Habitat
l2: Marshall Lew & Farzad Naeim, Los Angeles Tall Buildings Structural Design Council
d: There are geologic and seismic hazards that threaten a smaller population in the urban habitat. These hazards include the effects of surface fault rupture, liquefaction, and landsliding. With proper precautions, the exposure to these hazards could be avoidable.

t: In Search for a Local Image in the Arab City: The Case of Kuwait City
l2: Omar Khattab, Kuwait University
d: In its earlier attempts to catch up with western modernity and civilisation, the Arab City has lost, whether intentionally or unintentionally, its local image. Since this image was greatly associated with backwardness and obsoleteness. And was regarded as unfit setting for modern activities and uses.

t: High Rise-High Density
l2: Richard Keating, Richard Keating Architecture
d: Twenty years ago during the extraordinary building boom of the 80’s, we saw a plethora of object buildings in the U.S. exalting the glories of demographically monochromatic corporate imagery. Celebrating power and wealth, these buildings presented a rich variety of materials and opulence in hopes of gaining a perception of permanence and a domination of success in each economic community in which they operated.

t: The Tallest Concrete Building in Shanghai, China – Plaza 66
l2: Richard L. Tomasetti, Dennis Poon & Ling-en Hsaio, Thornton Tomasetti
d: Plaza 66 is the latest addition to the skyline of Shanghai, China; with a height of 281.5 meters, it’s the tallest concrete building in the city. This project embodies the challenges of building in a seismic and typhoon area with poor foundation conditions and materials of relatively limited strength, and illustrates solutions developed to meet those challenges.

t: The Citigroup Centre At No. 2 Park Street, Sydney
l2: Mike Haysler, Hyder Consulting; Robert Facioni, Structural Design Solutions
d: This paper covers the history of this site, a notorious black hole for many years, features achieved working on a design and construct basis and some of the technical challenges facing the structural engineer.

t: Birmingham: Inventing a New Life for the 21st Century
l2: Rod Duncan; JM Architects
d: In 1998-99 Rod Duncan participated in an international professional exchange to Birmingham. Duncan writes on the valuable lessons from Birmingham, identifying the factors influencing its successes.

t: An Integrated Design Approach to the Environmental Performance of Buildings
l2: Massimo Colomban & Marc Zobec, Permasteelisa; Mikkel Kragh, Dow Corning
d: The present paper deals with the complex relationship between architectural intensions, indoor environment, building envelope and environmental systems, energy performance and design process. The development of architecture and the construction industry is discussed with focus on the way design used to be adapted to, and responsive to, local climatic conditions and later on, with commercialisation neglected these aspects and relied on mechanical systems for fully air conditioned buildings.

t: New Towns in Hong Kong: Planning for the Next Generation
l2: Pun Chung Chan
d: Hong Kong is perhaps the city in this planet that is subject to the most intense development pressures.The purpose of this paper is, therefore, to give a brief introduction of the planning of our latest generation new towns which are now almost near completion.

t: Is There an Afterlife for the New Asian City?
l2: Stuart Brogan, Site Concepts International
d: This paper is a response to what Stuart Brogan sees happening in planning from the perspective of a designer of new communities and urban centres in varying environments, both in Australia and Asia. What are some important issues in the design of our future cities, our urban habitats, what should be some of the prime objectives of the designers?

t: Aurora Place Commercial Office Tower – 88 Phillip Street, Sydney
l2: Rocco Bressi, Bovis Lend Lease
d: This paper deals primarily with the structural design aspects of the commercial office tower.

t: BUILDING SYSTEMS AND CONCEPTS - Structural Innovation
l2: William F. Baker, SOM
d: Mankind had always lived close to the ground. During the thousands of years that preceded the birth of the skyscraper, life was limited by a reality of stairs and ladders. It was only after Elisha Otis invented a safer elevator that architects and engineers started a race to the sky with the 12 story Home Insurance Building – taking office buildings from the historical range of 5 or 6 stories to the 102-story Empire State Building in merely 40 years.

t: Cities Spanning the Millennia: Cairo/Alexandria
l2: Abdalla Abdelaziz Attia, Ain Shams Unviersity
d: The paper calls for the re-assessment of the national urban development policy in order to redistribute the new towns and new urban communities throughout the whole country so as to release the pressure on Greater Cairo Region from the growing urban concentration.

t: Asia Report
l2: Raymond Bates, Hong Kong Housing Authority
d:
Raymond Bates concentrates  on China and the Special Administrative Region of Hong Kong. This report will focus on four
areas: (a) The Asia economic crisis and its impact on development; (b) The demographics of the region; (c) “Bread and Butter” tall buildings; and (d) The “darker side of construction”.

t: A Report on Africa
l2: Isaac. G. Wanjohi, CTBUH, African Region
d: Africa is a very large continent with not only diverse cultural heritages, climatic features and economic characteristics but also different state administrations and governments. There are currently over 40 sovereign states in Africa with hardly any effective professional communication and dialogue among them.

t: Tall Building Design Innovations in Australia
l2: Brian Dean, Owen Martin, David Emery & Peter Chancellor
d: This paper describes three examples of innovative tall building designs undertaken by the authors' firm in Australasia. These involve the design of building structures up to 88-sotreys and as a mulch-discipline group, the firm's innovations have embraced structural, M&E, facade and fire engineering disciplines.

t: The Cyber Cities of Malaysia: Realising the Vision
l2: Azman bin Haji Awang, Technitium
d: At the threshold of the New Millennium, we face a critical challenge. And Malaysia is no exception. The challenge is to ensure that Malaysia remains competitive in the 21st Century; a challenge that is essential if we are to maintain the rapid economic growth, political stability and racial harmony we have long enjoyed. Malaysia has taken an innovative step forward to create one of the most wired regions in this part of the world by setting up the Multi-media Super Corridor (in short MSC or a Smart region).

t: The Sustainable Tall Building of the Third Millennium
l2: Harry Blutstein & Allan Rodger, RMIT University
d: The paper explores issues that will need to be resolved for a successful sustainable tall building of the Third Millennium. This will not just incorporate sustainable features, like energy efficiency, but a more fundamental change in the systems – technical, economic, organisational – involved in delivering the project, not to mention re-defining the project itself. The role of externalities also needs to be addressed.

t: Two Towers
l2: Phil Castillo, JAHN
d: This paper studies the two towers, Duetsche Post in Bonn and the MAX in Frankfurt, and how they rethink the typology of the high-rise tower in relation to function, technology and user comfort.

t: Aerodynamic Solutions to Minimize the Wind-Induced Dynamic Response of Tall Buildings
l2: W. H. Melbourne and J. C. K. Cheung
d: This paper gives guidelines to the way in which the shape or configuration of a building may be directed to achieve reductions in the cross-wind force spectrum and hence reductions in acceleration response to meet occupancy comfort criteria.

t: A Report on Oceania
l2: Henry J. Cowan, University of Sydney
d: Until the mid-19th century few buildings in Australia were higher than two storeys. This changed after the Gold Rush of 1851. By 1880 the population of Melbourne had increased from 20 000 to more than half a million. It had become the third largest city of the British Empire, and the headquarters of most Australian banks and important commercial enterprises. But even with this increase in population density few buildings exceeded four stories, because it was considered that people would not climb more stairs.

t: The Urban Habitat After Globalization: Cities in the Age of Climate Change and Fossil Fuel Depletion
l2: Peter Droege,  University of Newcastle
d: Two daunting challenges confront the world’s cities and city regions well within this coming generation, affecting the global urban system and human civilization as a whole: fossil fuel depletion and man-made catastrophic climate change. If these are not swiftly and effectively met their impacts will deeply affect all industrial, world and mega-city systems – and hit hard the fast-growing, major urban agglomerations of the developing world, along with their economies. Tall buildings carry a special significance in this context.

t: Shaping the Future of Human Settlements: Globalisation, and the Leadership and Management of Change
l2: Peter Ellyard, Preferred Futures, Universal Greening Group of Companies
d: Most of our human settlements: cities, and urban and rural communities are over managed and under led. As the interconnectedness of globalisation grows, market forces are increasingly shaping the forms and functions of human settlements. Vision and strategic intervention have lessened in importance and management has grown at the expense of leadership. Leadership has not been reinvented to operate in an interconnected globalisated planet. However the tools of leadership that have worked in the past are increasingly ineffective in a globalising interconnected world and marketplace of the 21st century. There has not been enough consideration about how the development of human settlements can be shaped and their habitability improved in an era of open global markets and trade, and of increasingly unempowered governments.

t: The Behaviour of Simple Non-Linear Tuned Mass Dampers
l2: Barry J. Vickery, University of Western Ontario; Jon K. Galsworthy, RWDI; Rafik Gerges, HSA & Associates
d: The present paper deals with the behaviour of non-linear T.M.D.’s subject to random Gaussian excitation. Attention is limited to two simple non-linear forms that occur commonly in practice. These two forms are dry-friction or constant force dampers and “velocity squared” or V2 damping associated with, for example, flow through an orifice or flow in a rough pipe.

t: Performance Code Requirements in the Tall Building Environment
l2: Robert E. Solomon & Brian Hagglund, National Fire Protection Association
d: The US code system is now moving to incorporate performance design options as an alternative to the long established, time tested, prescriptive design that has dominated fire protection codes and standards for so many years. Numerous NFPA Technical Committees are moving to incorporate this design approach into the codes and standards that they author. This paper will provide a background on the unique issues surrounding high-rise fire protection and how the future solutions to this problem can potentially be handled through application of performance based design.

t: Energy-Efficient Elevators for Tall Buildings
l2: Harri Hakala, Marja-Liisa Siikonen, Tapio Tyni & Jari Ylinen, Tampere University of Technology
d: According to elevator traffic studies, the traffic patterns in an office building, such as number of starts, round trips, and number of transported passengers, are repeated quite the same from day to day. In this study, the energy consumption of elevators in tall office buildings is studied by measurements and traffic simulations. A method using elevator load and travel distributions in calculating energy consumption is introduced. The simulation results are verified by measurements in a single-tenant office building. The proportions of savings gained by different drive systems and machinery as well as control systems are compared. A case study for yearly energy consumption in a tall building is presented.

t: Kidney Stones and The Structural Design of Tall Buildings
l2: Gary C. Hart, Weidlinger Associates
d: Structural Engineers are professionals in this “high tech” age just like doctors. They work for clients that really have no technical training or experience to evaluate their work except when the dreaded Kidney Stone, or the Design Basis Earthquake or Wind occurs. In the structural engineers natural hazard world, it hopefully will never occur. Fortunately for structural engineers, the theory of structural reliability was introduced in the 1960’s and this introduction brought order to structural engineering. It enabled the structural engineer to communicate with his or her client in the same way that the doctor of the author communicated with him.

t: Earthquakes and Tall Buildings – a Review
l2: Graham L. Hutchinson & Nelson T. K. Lam, University of Melbourne; John L. Wilson, Swinburne University of Technology
d: This paper has provided an overview of both the philosophy and methods used in the design and analysis of building structures to earthquake ground shaking. In particular, the following topics and issues were discussed : (a) seismic hazard and soil response modelling, (b) aseismic design methods, (c) design and detailing of structural systems and (d) future directions for seismic design.

t: Greening Office Towers
l2: Christoph Ingenhoven, Ingenhoven Overdiek und Partner
d: The aim in all the projects described in this paper is to work closely with engineers and other specialists throughout the whole of the design process. Internal questionnaires are used to measure the efficiency, the ecological consciousness, the economy of resource usage and the buildability of every project. This creates an architecture that is characterised by technological innovation and produces buildings appropriate to people’s working and leisure needs. It does not spring from artistic requirements alone but is rather a reflection of the architect’s responsibility for the environment.

t: New CTBUH Monograph on Building Motion, Perception and Mitigation
l2: Nicholas Isyumov & Tony Tschanz, Boundary Layer Wind Tunnel Laboratory
d: This paper provides an overview of the new Monograph which deals with motions experienced by tall buildings and their impact on design. Of various types of building motions wind-induced vibrations are of greatest concern to designers since they can occur relatively frequently and are difficult to control and mitigate.

t: Social/Community Impacts and Implications
l2: Paul Jerome, Department of Infrastructure Victoria
d:
For cities in many parts of the world, the dominant issues for the 21st century arise from continued urbanisation and the difficulties of providing basic infrastructure services and mass housing. However, for cities in the developed world – whilst still concerned with sustainable provision of basic services – a new dynamic is emerging.
